Discover How SILK Is Made From MILLIONS of SILKWORMS🐛| Cocoon Harvesting

Ever since human being invented clothes, a variety of fibres  have been discovered or invented to suit changes in time climate and fashion. Silk is one out of many fabrics that have been invoked over millennia despite drastic changes in our taste. Since it was discovered fine strong lustrous silk has been wooing the world.

The cultivation of silk and the craft of silk weaving are even more fascinating we thought it would be interesting to get an insight into the story of a silk thread, let's begin with the backbone of sericulture the silkworm. Commercial silk is obtained by cultivating different species of silkworm the most widely and most commercially used species is Bombyx Mori native to Asia. Silkworm are soft bodied and slow-moving and relatively fast growing insects like other insects silkworm to go through four stages of development egg, Larva, pupa and adult. The larva is a caterpillar, the pupa is what the silkworm changes into after spinning its cocoon before emerging as a month.

title: Crafting Fragrant Cloud Silk | 1,500-Year-Old Chinese Textile | Heritage Silk Dyeing Process
description:
Step into the world of Fragrant Cloud Silk (Xiangyunsha) — a rare and ancient Chinese textile with a history of over 1,500 years. 🌸🧵

This unique silk is created through a labor-intensive natural dyeing process:

Mulberry silk is dyed with gambier juice 🌿

Covered in iron-rich mud from the Pearl River 🏞️

Sun-dried and washed repeatedly until it reveals its distinctive two-toned luster ✨

Known for its cooling feel, durability, and subtle fragrance, Fragrant Cloud Silk is treasured in traditional garments and remains one of the finest examples of intangible cultural heritage textile art.

👉 Perfect for those interested in:

Ancient Chinese silk traditions

Natural dyeing techniques

Heritage textiles & fabrics

Mud-dyed silk (Xiangyunsha)

Intangible cultural heritage

title: The 5,000 Year Old Secret to Making Silk by Hand
description:
Step into a world where time slows down. This isn't a factory; it's a masterclass in a nearly lost art. This video documents the entire, painstaking process of creating pure silk from scratch, following a tradition that has been passed down for thousands of years.
Follow a master craftsman as he transforms thousands of delicate silkworm cocoons into a shimmering piece of fabric. You will witness every crucial step:
The boiling of the cocoons to release the filament.
The incredible skill of reeling a single, unbroken thread.
The rhythmic work of the handmade spinning wheel to create a strong yarn.
The meticulous alignment of thousands of threads on the warp.
The magic of weaving on a traditional, hand-powered loom.
The ancient secret of using natural wood ash to give the silk its legendary softness.
This is more than just a craft; it's a testament to patience, skill, and the profound connection between nature and human hands. You will never look at a piece of fabric the same way again.

description:

Video Description:

Discover the fascinating world of silkworm farming in China, where billions of silkworms are carefully raised to produce some of the finest silk in the world. This video takes you inside a silk cocoon harvesting and processing factory, showcasing the intricate process from silkworm to luxurious silk. Watch as we explore the entire journey, from the delicate care of the silkworms to the meticulous extraction of silk in state-of-the-art factories. Learn how traditional practices are combined with modern technology to create silk that is treasured worldwide.

Hashtags: #SilkwormFarm #SilkProduction #SilkCocoonHarvest #SilkwormProcessing #ChineseSilk #SilkFarming #Sericulture #SilkMaking #FarmingTechnology
Silk farming, also known as sericulture, plays a crucial role in modern agriculture and has deep roots in history. Silkworm farming is the foundation of this industry, with silk worms being raised on silk farms around the world, particularly in India and China. The life cycle of silkworms begins with their silkworm cocoon formation, which is then harvested in a mass harvest process. The silk making process involves extracting raw silk from silk worms, processing it in a silk processing factory, and ultimately producing fine silk threads.

In India, silk farming has a long tradition, where silk cocoon farming and the silk manufacturing process are integral to the agriculture industry. Modern techniques, such as silkworm automation and advanced farming technology, have improved efficiency in the production of silk from silk worms. Silkworm farming techniques have evolved, allowing for silkworm farming at home and even on noal farms, contributing to the silkworm farming business.

In China, silk farming is also a major agricultural activity, with specialized silk farms dedicated to mulberry cultivation for feeding silkworms. Silk processing involves multiple steps, including silkworm cocoon processing, and the final product is valued globally. Silkworms making silk is a fascinating process, and innovations in sericulture solutions continue to emerge, enhancing the silk manufacturing industry.

Silkworms are not just limited to traditional farms; in the gaming world, they even appear in V Rising gameplay, where players can find & farm silkworms and learn how to get silk in the game. This aspect of V Rising adds an educational twist to the gameplay and offers insight into the silkworm lifecycle.

The silk cocoon harvesting process is intricate, involving harvesting technology and skilled farm workers who understand how silkworms make cocoons and how to extract the finest silk. The journey from silkworm to silk is an ancient art, as silk is made from silkworms through a meticulous sericulture process that has been refined over centuries.

In the U.S., American agriculture has also seen an interest in silkworm farming, with a focus on integrating modern agricultural technology. The silkworm is known for its high silkworm nutrition facts, which are vital for producing strong, high-quality silk. Silkworm cocoon facts highlight the importance of the pupa stage in the silkworm lifecycle, which is crucial for producing the finest silk cocoon.

The silk making process in India and silk farming in China showcase the global impact of this ancient practice. Whether through silkworm farms, agriculture central initiatives, or new innovations like the new silk production technique, the story of silk farming continues to evolve. Silkworm history facts reveal how deeply ingrained this practice is in our culture, from the fields of felicia farms to the high-tech processing factories of today.

As we look to the future, the silkworm remains a symbol of the intersection between tradition and modern agriculture, with ongoing developments in sericulture promising even greater advancements in the silk farming industry.

title: Millions Earned from Silkworms in China 🤯
description:
Millions Earned from Silkworms in China 🤯

#shorts

Did you know?
In China, people are earning millions of dollars by farming silkworms!
They raise thousands — even millions — of silkworms on their farms.
Every day, the worms are fed fresh mulberry leaves, which help them grow faster.
Once they’re fully grown, they are placed in special nets, where they spin cocoons in just a few days.
These cocoons are then collected and boiled in hot water to extract fine silk threads.
The silk is then spun and woven —
and transformed into luxurious, soft, and valuable quilts.
These quilts are sold in international markets for millions of dollars!

title: How Mountains Of Worm Cocoons Are Turned Into Expensive Silk In Vietnam | Big Business
description:
Silk craft villages have dotted Vietnam for hundreds of years. They make silk by hand in a tedious 30-step process. Craftspeople feed the silkworms every four hours so they spin brilliantly yellow cocoons. And turning those cocoons into silk is even more delicate work.

In the 2010s, as cheaper, fake silk flooded the market and young textile workers moved to the big cities, this local craft started dying out. But one village turned things around, banding together into one cooperative called HanhSilk.

We head to northern Vietnam to learn how Nam Cao village saved its big business of silk.

title: Silkworm breeding reaps profits for farmers in southwest China
description:
Subscribe to our YouTube channel for free here:
https://sc.mp/subscribe-youtube

The cultivation of silkworm to produce silk, or sericulture, has become a lucrative industry in the city of Bijie in China’s southwest Guizhou province. About 40 people work at a sericulture cooperative, each earning a monthly salary of up to 4,000 yuan (US$593). In efforts to develop the region's silkworm breeding industry, 120 hectares (297 acres) of land have been set aside by the government for growing mulberry trees, the only source of food for silkworms.

title: The Cultural Heritage Wooden Weaving Loom Mechanism: Traditional Chinese Silkworm & Weaving Process
description:
"Wood, rhythm, and legacy, the holy trinity of traditional craftsmanship."

This video captures the mesmerizing operation of a wooden weaving loom, an intangible cultural heritage tool used in traditional Chinese silkworm silk production. Every movement of the shuttle, pedal, and warp thread reflects centuries of perfected technique, turning raw silk into elegant fabric with both patience and precision.

The Process:
Loom Preparation:

The loom’s wooden frame is carefully inspected and oiled to ensure smooth movement.

Warp threads are aligned and tensioned across the frame, forming the base for weaving.

Silk Handling:

Silkworm cocoons are harvested and boiled to extract delicate silk threads.

Threads are combed and guided onto the loom in a precise order to maintain uniformity.

Weaving Action:

The weaver manipulates pedals and heddles to lift selected warp threads.

The shuttle carries the weft thread through lifted warp threads rhythmically.

Repeated passes gradually form intricate patterns, designs, or functional silk cloth.

Finishing Touches:

Completed silk pieces are carefully cut, inspected, and pressed.

Loose threads are trimmed, and the loom is cleaned and maintained for future use.

Why This Works:
✔ Precision Crafting: Every warp and weft thread placement ensures durability and beauty.
✔ Cultural Preservation: The loom embodies centuries of knowledge, technique, and heritage.
✔ Sustainable Practice: Uses natural materials and manual energy, reducing environmental impact.

Key Tips:
⚠ Maintain consistent warp tension to prevent uneven weaving.
⚠ Handle silk threads gently to avoid breakage.
⚠ Regularly oil moving wooden parts for smooth, long-lasting operation.

Final Thought:
“Every rhythmic motion on this loom tells a story of human patience, ingenuity, and cultural continuity—proving that true craftsmanship transcends generations.”

UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00197
Description: Sericulture and silk craftsmanship of China, based in Zhejiang and Jiangsu Provinces near Shanghai and Chengdu in Sichuan Province, have an ancient history. Traditionally an important role for women in the economy of rural regions, silk-making encompasses planting mulberry, raising silkworms, unreeling silk, making thread, and designing and weaving fabric. It has been handed down within families and through apprenticeship, with techniques often spreading within local groups. The life cycle of the silkworm was seen as representing the life, death and rebirth of human beings. In the ponds that dot the villages, silkworm waste is fed to fishes, while mud from the ponds fertilizes the mulberry trees, and the leaves in turn feed the silkworms. Near the beginning of the lunar year, silkworm farmers invite artisans into their homes to perform the story of the Goddess of the Silkworm, to ward off evil and ensure a bountiful harvest. Every April, female silkworm farmers adorn themselves with colourful flowers made of silk or paper and make harvest offerings as part of the Silkworm Flower festival. Silk touches the lives of rural Chinese in more material ways, too, in the form of the silk clothes, quilts, umbrellas, fans and flowers that punctuate everyday life.

title: Collecting silkworm cocoons: smart frame for high-quality silk cocoon harvesting
description: This video shows in a well-organized sericulture farm, silkworm cocoons are collected using smart frames designed for efficiency and quality. The silkworms spin their cocoons directly on these frames, which are easy to handle and prevent damage to the delicate silk. Once the cocoons are fully formed, workers carefully lift the frames, gently brushing off any loose material, and transfer the intact cocoons into collection trays. The smart frame design ensures minimal handling, preserves the cocoon’s shape, and reduces waste, resulting in high-quality silk ready for processing. This method combines traditional care with modern efficiency, making cocoon harvesting faster, precise, and less labor-intensive.

title: Silkworm making Cocoon | Bombyx Mori | Sericulture
description:
The cocoon construction by a silkworm involves several stages, typically over two to three days:

1.Spinning Silk : Once the silkworm reaches the mature larval stage, it secretes a fluid silk protein from its silk glands. This fluid hardens when exposed to air, forming silk threads.

2. Forming the Cocoon : The silkworm attaches these silk threads to a surface and begins spinning a continuous thread around itself. It uses figure-eight movements of its head to form the cocoon, which is made of a single, long, unbroken silk fiber.

3. Complete Cocoon : After about two to three days, the silkworm completes the construction, fully enclosing itself inside the cocoon. This cocoon serves as a protective covering as the silkworm undergoes metamorphosis, transforming into a moth.

Once the process is complete, the cocoon provides a source of raw silk, which is harvested in silk production.

title: Traditional Silk Reeling Process: From Cocoon to Raw Silk Thread by Hand
description:
Watch this incredible video showcasing the traditional process of making raw silk thread from silkworm cocoons. This ancient craft involves several precise steps done entirely by hand.

The process begins with cooking the cocoons in hot water to soften the sericin—the natural gum that binds the silk filament. The loose filament ends are then collected and skillfully reeled onto a Charkha reeling machine to form a continuous thread. The reeler carefully adds more cocoons to ensure the silk thread maintains a consistent denier (thickness).

Finally, the raw silk is boiled again with an alkali (soda) in a process called degumming to completely remove the sericin, leaving the fiber soft, lustrous, and ready for weaving. This is a beautiful glimpse into the authentic handicraft and sericulture traditions of silk production.

project_name_en: Sericulture and silk craftsmanship of China
title: The Sericulture and Silk Craftsmanship of China
description:
Spanning at least 5,000 years,
        silk-weaving craftsmanship has woven out delicate fabrics as well as splendid and profound Chinese culture, with warps and wefts.
        Sericulture originated in China.
        Legend has it that over 5,000 years ago,
        Empress Leizu, wife of the ancient Yellow Emperor,
        managed to domesticate silkworms
        and produce silk thread from the filaments.
        The knowledge of sericulture then became widespread.
        As a cultural symbol of China, silk craftsmanship
        has exerted significant influence on human civilization.
        Silk-making encompasses various processes, including mulberry planting, silkworm raising, silk unreeling, dyeing and weaving.
        Silk-making has been handed down and developed for thousands of years,
        with various techniques spreading within local groups,
        each demonstrating unique characteristics.
        The Yuhang Rinsing Silk Wadding in east China’s Zhejiang Province managed to develop white and pure silk;
        The Hangluo silk is soft, smooth and tenacious；
        The Shuanglin silk damask is as thin as a cicada's wings and as light as the mist,
        whereas silk from north China’s Shanxi Province is bright and colorful, a tribute to the imperial court.
        All local styles constitute the unique silk culture of the Chinese nation.
        The skills of silk-making eventually spread beyond China.
        Goods and thoughts from different civilizations
        travelled the ancient Silk Road,
        which connected China and the Far East with the Middle East and Europe.
        Fine silk sketches the splendid image that is exclusive to the Chinese nation.

title: How Silkworms Make Silk
description:
How Silkworms Make Silk

Look. These are silkworms gnawing on mulberry leaves.
The cultivation of silkworms to produce silk is called sericulture, or silk farming.
Cultivating healthy mulberry trees is crucial since silkworms feed exclusively on them. What we’re seeing here are silkworm eggs, carefully collected and kept in controlled environments to ensure proper hatching.

title: Sericulture and silk craftsmanship of China
description:
China is the world's largest and earliest silk producer. The vast majority of Chinese silk originates from the mulberry silkworms (Bombyx mori). During the larval stage of its life-cycle, the insects feed on the leaves of mulberry trees. Non-mulberry silkworms cocoon production in China primarily focuses on wild silk from the Chinese Tussah moth (Antheraea spp.). This moth typically feeds on trees (e.g. oaks) and its larvae spin coarser, flatter, yellower filament than the mulberry silk moths.
In 2005, China accounted for 74 percent of the global raw silk production and 90 percent of the world export market.

title: Tussar Silk, The Story Of No-Violence Silk From India
description:
Tussar Silk is produced mainly in the eastern regions of India including Bihar, Jharkhand, Odisha, Chhattisgarh, and West Bengal.

It is also called ‘Wild Silk’. And there is a specific reason for it. These silkworms (Antheraea) are not domesticated creatures. They feed on wild trees instead of being confined to a little farm or an overly controlled environment.

There are two types of Tussar silk. Pure Tussar and Tussar Ghica. Pure Tussar is made using undamaged cocoons, which means the larvae die inside. On the other hand, Tussar Ghicha is the one in which the moth makes a hole at the top of the cocoon and breaks out and therefore unharmed. The moth flies out, mates, and produces more eggs; more importantly, it’s free!

This is why Tussar Silk has earned the name “No-Violence-Silk”. This is also why there has been a renewed interest in it by designers and textile enthusiasts as it offers ethical practices in fashion and it’s environmentally friendly. Even the trees on which the silkworms feed are not damaged or cut down. It is to be noted that most of the villagers don’t have the concept of violence or non-violence silk. For them, it is a modern fancy term. They have been making Tussar Ghicha silk for centuries.

Also watch: Pisanki, The Egg-cellent Polish Tradition: https://youtu.be/ScEtEqcA4ZM

description:
Sericulture, the cultivation of silkworms to produce silk, was a closely guarded Chinese secret for thousands of years. From the 6th century, silkworms - and their main food source - the white mulberry tree were moved along the Silk Roads to Constantinople, the capital of the Byzantine Empire.

By the 12th century Italian cities like Venice, Lucca, and Florence developed their own thriving silk industries. Today CREE-AA, Europe's largest facility for silkworm research, is exploring new uses for silk, from cosmetics to pharmaceuticals.

SUMMARY
=======
China is the world's largest silk producer. The vast majority of Chinese silk originates from the mulberry silkworms (Bombyx mori). During the larval stage of its life-cycle, the insects feeds on the leaves of mulberry trees. Non-mulberry silkworms cocoon production in China primarily focuses on wild silk from the Chinese Tussah moth (Antheraea spp.). This moth typically feeds on trees (e.g. oaks) and its larvae spin coarser, flatter, yellower filament than the mulberry silk moths.In 2005, China accounted for 74 percent of the global raw silk production and 90 percent of the world export market.

description:
hf259. SilkWorm Farm - How Billions of SilkWorm for silk Chinese - Food Factory

Silk worm farms are truly fascinating food factories in miniature. Billions of silkworms, mere specks at first, transform into ravenous eaters within a dedicated mulberry leaf processing facility.  These tiny silk-producing caterpillars become the stars of the show, consuming vast quantities of fresh mulberry leaves throughout their rapid growth phase.  Workers meticulously manage this miniature food factory, ensuring a constant supply of leaves to keep the silkworms producing silk.  After a few weeks, the well-fed silkworms spin their cocoons, another tiny marvel of this biological food factory.  The final stage sees the cocoons unwound, their silk threads carefully collected - a testament to the incredible efficiency of this natural food factory.

title: How the Chinese Turn Cocoons into Luxury Fabric? #shorts #silk #weaving
description:
Light as air and softly textured, raw silk gauze is one of the most delicate silk fabrics ever made. Woven from minimally processed silk threads, it keeps its natural strength, softness, and subtle sheen.

🇨🇳 China – Traditional Silk Regions
Raw silk gauze is mainly found in historic silk-producing areas such as Suzhou, Hangzhou, and Sichuan. These regions have produced fine silk for thousands of years using hand-operated looms.

🏞️ Sericulture Villages
In rural villages where silkworms are raised, raw silk is spun and woven locally. Gauze weaving requires extreme skill, as the threads are thin and easily broken.

🧵 Handloom Weaving Workshops
Authentic raw silk gauze is created in small artisan workshops, where weavers control tension by hand to achieve the fabric’s airy, transparent structure.

Raw silk gauze isn’t just fabric—it’s patience, skill, and centuries of textile history woven into something almost weightless.

How Did China Protect The Secret Of Silk Production? In this informative video, we will unravel the fascinating history behind silk production in ancient China and the various methods used to protect its secrets. The art of sericulture was not merely a craft; it was a vital component of China's economy and cultural identity. We will discuss the intricate processes involved in silk production, from the cultivation of mulberry trees to the careful reeling of silk fibers.

You’ll learn about the geographical measures that kept this knowledge confined to specific regions and the strict penalties imposed on those who dared to share these secrets with outsiders. Additionally, we will explore the cultural narratives, such as the story of Empress Leizu, that reinforced the importance of silk in Chinese society and obscured its origins.

Through archaeological discoveries, we will highlight the significance of silk in ancient China, showcasing artifacts that provide evidence of its longstanding presence. By examining these historical practices, we can gain a clearer understanding of the social structures and technological advancements of ancient civilizations.

title: How silk cloth is made | sericulture | life cycle of silkworm | silkworm farm
description:
How silk cloth is made | sericulture | life cycle of silkworm | silkworm farm

The life cycle of a silkworm, also known as Bombyx mori, consists of four stages:

Stage 1: Egg
- Female silkworms lay eggs on mulberry leaves.
- Eggs hatch into larvae within 3-4 days.

Stage 2: Larva (Caterpillar)
- Larvae feed on mulberry leaves and molt four times.
- This stage lasts around 20-30 days.

Stage 3: Pupa (Cocoon)
- When fully grown, larvae spin a protective cocoon around themselves.
- Inside the cocoon, the larva undergoes metamorphosis, transforming into a pupa.
- This stage lasts around 7-10 days.

Stage 4: Adult (Moth)
- The adult moth emerges from the cocoon.
- Moths mate and lay eggs to start the cycle again.
- Adult moths live for around 5-7 days.

Silkworms are cultivated for their silk, which is harvested by soaking the cocoons in hot water and unwinding the silk fibers.

title: the history of silk production in china
description:
Silk production originated in Neolithic China, dating back to around 4000 BCE, with early woven fragments discovered from as early as 2700 BCE. The secret of sericulture (raising silkworms) was a closely guarded state secret for thousands of years, but the technology eventually spread east to Korea and Japan, and then west to Europe around 552 CE. Silk became a major source of income and a valuable commodity, leading to the creation of the Silk Road trade network.
This video explains the history of silk in ancient China:
Related video thumbnail
54s


The Ancient Library
YouTube • 14 Feb 2022
Early origins and development
Neolithic China: The earliest evidence of silk production comes from the Neolithic Yangshao culture (c. 4000 BCE) and later findings at sites like Qianshanyang, where woven silk fragments were discovered.
Legendary beginnings: Legend attributes the invention of silk production to the wife of the Yellow Emperor, Lei Zu, who supposedly discovered the process after a silkworm cocoon fell into her tea.
Exclusive use: Initially, silk was reserved exclusively for the emperor, the court, and high-ranking officials, with strict dress codes dictating who could wear certain colors.
Economic and cultural importance: As production techniques advanced, silk became a vital part of the economy, used for paying taxes, wages, and as a diplomatic gift.
Spread of silk production
Eastward expansion: Silk technology first spread eastward to Korea and Japan a few centuries before the Common Era.
Westward migration: The secret was not widely known outside of China until 552 CE when Nestorian monks smuggled silkworm eggs to the Byzantine Empire.
The Silk Road: The demand for Chinese silk drove the development of the Silk Road, a vast network of trade routes that connected China with the West and facilitated the exchange of not only goods but also ideas, culture, and religion.
You can watch this video to learn about the silk trade route:
Related video thumbnail
56s


OER Project
YouTube • 7 Jul 2021
Later history
Industrialization: While silk production was mechanized as early as 1090, the Industrial Revolution marked a significant shift towards fully industrialized production methods.
Continued production: The Ming and Qing dynasties continued to produce high-quality silk, with cities like Nanjing, Suzhou, and Hangzhou becoming major centers for imperial silk production.

One fascinating fact about silkworms and sericulture is that the production of silk dates back over 5,000 years to ancient China. [Transcript]
Have you ever draped yourself in worm vomit?

The answer is yes if you have worn silk!

Thousands of years ago, The Chinese Empress Leizu was sitting beneath a mulberry tree, when a cocoon from a silkworm fell into her tea. As she tried to remove the cocoon, she noticed that the silk thread that it was made from began to unravel and unwind. Fascinated by the fine, delicate thread, she had an idea.

And that idea was a little something called sericulture.

and what is sericulture you may ask?

Well, it’s the practice of gathering roughly 3000 silkworms to make cocoons, so that they can be boiled alive inside of them so that humans can harvest their silk make sexy lingerie.

title: Inside China’s Billion-Dollar Silk Empire: Farming 100,000 Tons of Silk Per Year
description:
Did you know China produces 80% of the world's silk? Step inside the fascinating world of industrial sericulture. This video documents the incredible journey of silk production, blending ancient traditions with modern technology.

We take you from the misty mulberry fields to the high-tech factory floors to show exactly how billions of silkworms are raised, fed, and harvested to create one of the most valuable materials on Earth.

In this video, you will see: 🐛 The Lifecycle: From moths laying thousands of yellow eggs to the hatching of tiny larvae. 🍃 The Feeding: How farmers harvest tons of mulberry leaves to feed voracious silkworms that eat 50,000x their body weight. 🏭 The Technology: Automated feeding machines, climate-controlled rearing rooms, and lime powder disinfection. b The Harvest: Removing millions of cocoons from grid frames and sorting them by quality. 🧵 The Processing: Boiling cocoons to soften the sericin and the mesmerizing process of reeling fine silk threads. 🏥 The End Product: From luxury textiles and silk quilts to surgical sutures and cosmetic soaps.

Witness the ingenuity of Chinese farmers and the scale of the "Lumi Farm" operations in this deep dive into the global silk industry.

title: The Origins And History Of Silk
description:
Silk is a natural protein fiber, some forms of which can be woven into textiles. The protein fiber of silk is composed mainly of fibroin and is produced by certain insect larvae to form cocoons. The best-known silk is obtained from the cocoons of the larvae of the mulberry silkworm Bombyx mori reared in captivity (sericulture). The shimmering appearance of silk is due to the triangular prism-like structure of the silk fibre, which allows silk cloth to refract incoming light at different angles, thus producing different colors.

Silk is produced by several insects, but generally only the silk of moth caterpillars has been used for textile manufacturing. There has been some research into other types of silk, which differ at the molecular level. Silk is mainly produced by the larvae of insects undergoing complete metamorphosis, but some adult insects such as webspinners also produce silk, and some insects such as raspy crickets produce silk throughout their lives. Silk production also occurs in Hymenoptera (bees, wasps, and ants), silverfish, mayflies, thrips, leafhoppers, beetles, lacewings, fleas, flies, and midges. Other types of arthropod produce silk, most notably various arachnids such as spiders (see spider silk).

The word silk comes from Old English sioloc, from Greek σηρικός serikos, "silken", ultimately from an Asian source (cf. Chinese si "silk", Manchurian sirghe, Mongolian sirkek).

Several kinds of wild silk, which are produced by caterpillars other than the mulberry silkworm, have been known and used in China, South Asia, and Europe since ancient times. However, the scale of production was always far smaller than for cultivated silks. There are several reasons for this: firstly, they differ from the domesticated varieties in colour and texture and are therefore less uniform; secondly, cocoons gathered in the wild have usually had the pupa emerge from them before being discovered so the silk thread that makes up the cocoon has been torn into shorter lengths; and thirdly, many wild cocoons are covered in a mineral layer that stymies attempts to reel from them long strands of silk. Thus, previously,  the only way to obtain silk suitable for spinning into textiles in areas where commercial silks are not cultivated was by tedious and labor-intensive carding.

Commercial silks originate from reared silkworm pupae, which are bred to produce a white-colored silk thread with no mineral on the surface. The pupae are killed by either dipping them in boiling water before the adult moths emerge or by piercing them with a needle. These factors all contribute to the ability of the whole cocoon to be unravelled as one continuous thread, permitting a much stronger cloth to be woven from the silk. Wild silks also tend to be more difficult to dye than silk from the cultivated silkworm. A technique known as demineralizing allows the mineral layer around the cocoon of wild silk moths to be removed, leaving only variability in color as a barrier to creating a commercial silk industry based on wild silks in the parts of the world where wild silk moths thrive, such as in Africa and South America.

Genetic modification of domesticated silkworms is used to facilitate the production of more useful types of silk.

Silk fabric was first developed in ancient China. The earliest example of silk fabric is from 3630 BC, and it was used as wrapping for the body of a child from a Yangshao site in Qingtaicun at Xingyang, Henan.

Legend gives credit for developing silk to a Chinese empress, Leizu (Hsi-Ling-Shih, Lei-Tzu). Silks were originally reserved for the Emperors of China for their own use and gifts to others, but spread gradually through Chinese culture and trade both geographically and socially, and then to many regions of Asia. Because of its texture and lustre, silk rapidly became a popular luxury fabric in the many areas accessible to Chinese merchants. Silk was in great demand, and became a staple of pre-industrial international trade. In July 2007, archaeologists discovered intricately woven and dyed silk textiles in a tomb in Jiangxi province, dated to the Eastern Zhou Dynasty roughly 2,500 years ago. Although historians have suspected a long history of a formative textile industry in ancient China, this find of silk textiles employing "complicated techniques" of weaving and dyeing provides direct evidence for silks dating before the Mawangdui-discovery and other silks dating to the Han Dynasty (202 BC-220 AD).

title: Ancient China's Silk Monopoly..💰🌏🐛
description:
For over three thousand years ancient China possessed the most valuable manufacturing secret in human history, producing a fabric so extraordinary that the entire civilized world was willing to pay almost any price to obtain it. 🌏✨ The Roman Empire was so consumed by its desire for silk that it drained its treasury importing it, with Roman senators eventually passing laws attempting to ban men from wearing it because the obsession had become an economic crisis. 👘💰 China protected its monopoly with terrifying efficiency — spies were executed, borders were monitored, and the knowledge of sericulture was considered a state secret of the highest possible order, guarded as carefully as any military intelligence. 🔐⚔️ The Silk Road — the most famous trade network in human history — existed entirely because of this single Chinese monopoly, stretching thousands of miles across deserts and mountains just to move this one fabric from Chinese workshops to the hungry markets of the ancient world. 🐫🗺️ China maintained this extraordinary advantage for so long that by the time the secret finally escaped through Byzantine monks smuggling silkworm eggs in hollow walking sticks in 550 AD, they had already enjoyed three millennia of complete and total dominance over the ancient world's most precious commodity. 🐛🕵️

China’s Manufacturing Millions Of Tons Of Silk Are Woven Into The Most Exquisite Fabrics On Earth

China produces millions of tons of silk every year — yet most people have no idea how deeply this ancient craft still shapes modern manufacturing.

From tiny silkworms spinning fragile threads to industrial looms weaving some of the most luxurious fabrics on Earth, this is a process refined over thousands of years — and scaled to levels almost impossible to imagine.

Silk isn’t just fashion.
It’s history, chemistry, engineering, and patience — multiplied by millions.

Once you see how it’s made, you’ll never look at fabric the same way again.

China produces **millions of tons of silk**, turning an ancient process into one of the most precise and beautiful manufacturing systems on Earth — a hidden world of patience, engineering, and luxury few people ever see.

title: The Silkworm Factory: Thousands Crawling on Specialized Multi-Tier Racks in China 👀
description:
This video offers a mesmerizing and highly detailed look inside a modern, large-scale silkworm rearing facility in China. The footage reveals a specially designed room, likely part of a "smart silkworm factory," where temperature, humidity, and oxygen are finely controlled to optimize growth year-round .

The scene is dominated by an array of specialized, multi-tiered silkworm rearing racks. These are not simple wooden shelves; they are engineered structures, possibly "circulating" or movable frames designed for maximum space efficiency and ease of maintenance . Each rack is divided into multiple layers, and every layer is teeming with life.

Thousands of plump, pale silkworms, in their late larval stages, crawl and climb across the racks. They move in a slow, continuous wave, their countless legs creating a soft, rustling sound. They explore the edges of their rearing platforms, climb over each other, and continuously feed on the fresh mulberry leaves that have been spread across the surfaces. The racks are designed with features to contain the worms, such as "L-shaped protective plates" around the edges to prevent them from crawling out and escaping .

The room itself is bright and spacious, a stark contrast to traditional, labor-intensive methods . You might see automated systems for feeding or climate control in the background. The "special design" of the racks is evident in their construction, which often includes features for easy cleaning, waste removal (with collection plates beneath the rearing surfaces), and even mechanisms to prevent disease . Some advanced frames are "circulating," meaning they can move or rotate to allow farmers to access all silkworms easily without walking down narrow aisles .

This is not just a room with boxes; it is a finely tuned, vertical farming environment where every inch of space is utilized to cultivate millions of these valuable insects.

🐛 The Science of the Silkworm Rearing Room:

Vertical Farming Efficiency: Modern racks allow for multi-layer rearing, dramatically increasing the number of silkworms that can be raised per square meter of floor space . Some designs can feature 3-5 layers or more .

Designed for Climbing: The silkworms' natural instinct to climb is accommodated by the rack design. They move freely across the mesh or solid floors, and in some systems, are encouraged to climb onto special frames or "paper cocooning frames" when they are ready to spin their silk .

Integrated Waste Management: The racks are often built with a removable collection plate or drawer beneath the rearing surface . This "residue collection plate" catches silkworm feces (frass) and leftover leaf fragments, making cleaning much more efficient and hygienic.

Climate Control: The entire room is managed as a closed ecosystem. Intelligent systems regulate the environment, freeing farmers from the backbreaking labor of the past and allowing for multiple rearing cycles per year .

This footage provides a rare window into the industrialization of an ancient craft. It shows how technology transforms a delicate biological process into a highly efficient, year-round production system, while still allowing the silkworms to engage in their natural, crawling and climbing behaviors on specially engineered racks.

title: Chinese Silk Craftsmanship 中国丝绸技艺 | Little Classroom on Chinese Culture and Tourism (34)
description:
Delicate, lustrous, and soft to the touch. The ethereal fabric that we now call silk has threaded its way throughout China's history.

One cannot be certain of its origin, but the humble ancient Chinese people credited their own wisdom to Leizu, wife of the Yellow Emperor (the legendary ancestor of all Chinese people), as the inventor of sericulture.

Like almost all genres of art on the vast land of China, the style and texture of silk are also multifarious. Hangluo satin from Hangzhou, Zhejiang province, is renowned for its airy and sheer texture, while Yunjin brocade from Nanjing, Jiangsu province, a luxurious fabric often used for royal garments, represents China's silk weaving technique at its prime.

In the hands of Chinese artists, the thinnest threads can weave pictures of immense possibilities, and the softest material can traverse thousands of years. As one of the many marvels of ancient China, silk is not merely a type of textile. It is a cultural icon, and an embodiment of elegance and grace.

title: The Origins of Silk #silk making #youtubeshorts #trending
description:
The Origins of Silk
The story of silk begins in ancient China, where its discovery is attributed to Empress Leizu, the wife of the Yellow Emperor. Legend has it that around 2700 BCE, while drinking tea in her garden, a silkworm cocoon fell into her cup, unraveling to reveal the delicate threads. Intrigued by the strong yet soft fibers, she began experimenting with the threads, leading to the creation of the first silk garments. Thus, the secret of silk was born, guarded jealously by the Chinese for millennia.

Chinese silk became highly sought after across the ancient world, and the establishment of the Silk Road facilitated its trade far beyond China’s borders. This network of trade routes extended from China through Asia, reaching as far as the Middle East and Europe. Silk became a symbol of luxury and wealth, reserved for the elite and royalty. The allure of silk was so profound that it inspired the Roman Empire to trade gold and other valuable resources for the fabric, influencing economies and cultures alike.

The Silk Making Process
The creation of silk is an intricate and labor-intensive process that begins with the humble silkworm. Silkworms, specifically the Bombyx mori species, are raised in controlled environments to ensure their health and productivity. These worms feed exclusively on mulberry leaves, which provide the essential nutrients needed to spin their cocoons.

Once the silkworms reach maturity, they begin spinning their cocoons using a fluid secreted from their salivary glands. The fluid hardens upon exposure to air, forming a continuous thread that measures over a thousand meters in length. It takes about two to three days for a silkworm to complete its cocoon.

Harvesting and Reeling
The next stage in silk production is harvesting the cocoons. Timing is crucial, as the silkworms must be harvested before they metamorphose into moths, as the emerging moth would break the silk thread. The cocoons are carefully gathered and sorted based on their quality and size.

To unravel the silk fibers, the cocoons are subjected to a process called reeling. This involves immersing the cocoons in hot water to soften the sericin, a natural gum that holds the fibers together. Skilled artisans then unwind the filaments, combining several threads to create a single, strong silk strand. This process requires precision and expertise, as a consistent tension must be maintained to ensure the evenness of the thread.

Spinning and Weaving
Once the silk threads are reeled, they are spun into yarn. This process involves twisting the threads to create uniformity and strength. The silk yarn is then dyed using natural or synthetic dyes, depending on the desired color and finish.

Weaving is the final stage in transforming silk yarn into fabric. Weavers use looms to interlace the threads, creating various patterns and textures. Silk weaving is an art form in itself, with each culture and region having its own unique techniques and styles. From delicate brocades to intricate embroideries, silk fabric reflects the creativity and craftsmanship of the weavers.

Silk Around the World
While China is the birthplace of silk, other regions have developed their own silk-making traditions. In India, silk production is an ancient craft, with regions like Karnataka and West Bengal renowned for their vibrant silk sarees. The sericulture industry in India employs millions, contributing significantly to the country's economy.

In Japan, silk is an integral part of traditional arts, such as kimono-making and silk painting. The Japanese have perfected techniques like the production of habutai and chirimen silk, known for their fine texture and beauty. Similarly, Thailand is celebrated for its handwoven silks, particularly the intricate ikat and mudmee patterns.

The Evolution and Modernization of Silk Making
The industrial revolution brought significant changes to the silk industry. The introduction of mechanized looms and machinery streamlined the production process, increasing efficiency and output. Despite these advancements, the essence of traditional silk making remains, preserving the artistry and skill passed down through generations.

Today, silk continues to be a symbol of luxury and elegance, adorning high fashion collections and interior designs. Its versatility and eco-friendly nature have also led to innovations in technology and medicine, such as biodegradable sutures and silk-based biomaterials.

Conclusion
Silk making is a testament to human ingenuity and creativity, transforming nature’s simplest forms into something extraordinary. Its journey from a silkworm cocoon to a luxurious fabric embodies a deep connection between culture, craftsmanship, and history. As we continue to cherish silk for its beauty and significance, the story of silk making remains a legacy of elegance, resilience, and innovation, forever woven into the fabric of human civilization. #silk making #youtubeshorts #trendingnews #silk

title: SILK Road Espionage and the Secret That Slipped Out of China
description:
A state secret moved across Asia under conditions that should have made theft impossible. Its escape broke one of the most tightly protected monopolies in the ancient world and altered the balance between empires.

The Silk Road Espionage Network That Stole Chinas Greatest Secret examines how sericulture, the controlled production of silk, may have been transferred from China to Byzantium in the 6th century under Emperor Justinian I. Chinese law treated the export of silkworm eggs, mulberry stock, and production knowledge as a capital crime, while imperial workshops and compartmentalized labor helped preserve the monopoly that made silk a source of revenue, diplomacy, and prestige. The best-known account comes from Procopius, who described monks carrying silkworm eggs from the east, but many historians point to a broader covert network involving Sogdian traders, Nestorian contacts, caravan routes, and long-distance commercial intelligence. At stake was more than luxury cloth: Persian control of overland trade strained Byzantine finances, and domestic silk production promised strategic independence. From Bombyx mori and mulberry cultivation to palace workshops in Constantinople, the evidence reveals an early case of industrial espionage with lasting geopolitical consequences.

title: The Art of Sericulture: How Farmers Provide Perfect Frames for Silkworms 👀
description:
This video offers a fascinating look inside a modern silkworm farm, capturing the precise moment when a farmer prepares customized paper frame mountages (also called cocooning frames or 方格簇 fāng gé cù) for mature silkworms. These specialized structures are where the silkworms will spin their precious silk cocoons, and their design is critical to producing high-quality silk .

The Spinning Frames:

The farmer is shown handling what appear to be cardboard or paper-based grid frames, often called "Japanese-type" mountages or "box-type paper cocooning frames" . These frames consist of numerous individual cells or compartments, each one carefully sized to hold exactly one silkworm as it spins its cocoon . The standard cell dimensions are approximately 4.5 x 3.3 cm, providing the ideal space for a single cocoon .

Why Custom Frames Matter:

The design of these mountages is based on a deep understanding of silkworm behavior. Quality frames must meet several critical requirements :

Sufficient and equal space for each larva to spin without crowding

Proper ventilation and moisture absorption, as the frames are made from materials that help maintain the necessary microclimate

Accessible surfaces that make it easy for silkworms to crawl onto and position themselves

Durability and reusability for multiple rearing cycles

Some advanced designs use a "box-type" structure with cover plates that prevent silkworms from crawling out of their designated cells—a common problem with older-style frames . As described in one patent, "the silkworms cannot crawl around randomly and can only spin cocoons within their grid holes" .

The Farmer's Preparation:

The farmer in the video is likely performing several preparatory steps. This may involve :

Cleaning and disinfecting the frames to prevent disease

Arranging the mountages in a well-ventilated area with even lighting (bright light or dark shadows can cause crowding)

Positioning the frames slightly inclined so that silkworm waste and urine drop to the ground, preventing staining of the valuable cocoons

Ensuring proper spacing between frames to allow for air circulation and to prevent "double cocoons" where two worms spin together

The Moment of Transfer:

The video may show the farmer gently placing ripe silkworms—those that have stopped eating and become slightly translucent, raising their heads in search of a spinning spot—into the prepared frames . Each worm is guided into its own cell, where it will spend the next 2-3 days spinning a continuous silk filament up to 1,500 meters long.

The Result:

When done correctly, using high-quality mountages can result in over 80-90% of cocoons being graded as first quality—a crucial factor for producing premium silk . The structure ensures each cocoon is formed without defects, with consistent thickness and color.

A Blend of Tradition and Precision:

This practice, documented as far back as the early 1200s in Chinese paintings depicting sericulture, has evolved into a precise science . The farmer's careful preparation of these frames demonstrates the level of detail required in modern silk production—a process that transforms a simple paper grid into the birthplace of one of the world's most luxurious fibers.

Disclaimer: The mountages shown are standard equipment in sericulture. Proper preparation ensures the highest quality silk and the well-being of the silkworms during the spinning stage.

title: FSPDX - Silk and Sericulture:  Beauty Inspired by a Social Contract with Dr Ina Asim
description:
The digital exhibition titled “The Artful Fabric of Collecting” includes objects most collected by founder of the art museum, Gertrud Bass Warner, and focuses on those that reveal aspects of the role of collectors in preserving art. This recent humanities project is funded by the Mellon Foundation, connecting objects in the Jordan Schnitzer Museum of Art,  Special Collections and University Archives at the University of Oregon.

In this talk with First Saturday PDX,  Professor Ina Asim will show us how Gertrud Bass Warner collected precious silk textiles at a time when a sharp distinction was made between fine arts and textiles in the West, a categorization that only slowly recedes. She also collected printed illustrated manuals that reflect the seasonal works in agriculture and sericulture in traditional China. Such genre scenes had originally been commissioned and collected by China’s emperors, works of art intended to remind the ruler that his legacy hinged on his ability to protect a balanced society in which the two pillars of a flourishing economy were supported and allowed to thrive. The art of collecting with passion and insight has preserved silk textiles and the documentation of silk production for us. Contemporary technology helps to explore both categories of objects across physical distance unencumbered by the protective devices necessary to ensure their physical conservation.

More information and recommended reading on this subject can be found on our website: https://www.firstsaturdaypdx.org/silk-and-sericulture-beauty-inspired-by-a-social-contract.html

​About the speaker:
Ina Asim is Associate Professor of Chinese History at the University of Oregon. Her interest in archaeology and the history of China’s material culture developed from her work on Song dynasty tombs and the urban culture of pre-modern China. She has published often and has involvement in various film documentary projects.

title: The Story of Silk #Behind the Scenes : The Hardworking Chinese Process.The Journey of Silkworms
description:
Mulberry leaves are the sole food source for silkworms. Chinese farmers carefully grow and maintain mulberry plantations, ensuring the leaves are fresh and nutritious for silkworms.

Female moths lay thousands of eggs, which are collected by farmers.

Silkworms hatch and are fed a strict diet of mulberry leaves. Villagers often work tirelessly to feed them multiple times daily.

Over 2-3 days, silkworms spin their cocoons using a single silk thread that can be over 1,000 meters long.

Cocoons are collected just before the moths emerge, to preserve the long silk thread.

The cocoons are boiled to soften the sericin (a protein) and release the silk strands.

Explore the rich tapestry of Chinese silk craftsmanship with a captivating tour of the Zhongshan Silk Factory in China. The tour offers a sensory experience as you feel the smooth texture of freshly woven silk and witness vibrant colors coming to life through the dyeing process. Engage with artisans who proudly carry on the legacy of silk craftsmanship, and discover the significance of silk in Chinese culture, art, and fashion.

Cap off your visit with a visit to the factory's showroom, where you can explore a stunning array of silk products, from elegant garments and accessories to intricate home furnishings. Whether you're a connoisseur of fine textiles or a curious traveler seeking cultural enrichment, the Zhongshan Silk Factory tour promises a captivating and educational journey through the time-honored tradition of Chinese silk production.

project_name_en: Sericulture and silk craftsmanship of China
title: Chinese Traditional Silk Weaving
description:
The production of silk originates in China in the Neolithic (Yangshao culture, 4th millennium BCE). Silk remained confined to China until the Silk Road opened at some point during the later half of the first millennium BCE. China maintained its virtual monopoly over silk production for another thousand years. Not confined to clothing, silk was also used for a number of other applications, including writing, and the color of silk worn was an important indicator of social class during the Tang Dynasty.

Silk cultivation spread to Japan around 300 CE, and, by 522 CE, the Byzantines managed to obtain silkworm eggs and were able to begin silkworm cultivation. The Arabs also began to manufacture silk during this same time. As a result of the spread of sericulture, Chinese silk exports became less important, although they still maintained dominance over the luxury silk market. The Crusades brought silk production to Western Europe, in particular to many Italian states, which saw an economic boom exporting silk to the rest of Europe. Changes in manufacturing techniques also began to take place during the Middle Ages, with devices such as the spinning wheel first appearing. During the 16th century France joined Italy in developing a successful silk trade, though the efforts of most other nations to develop a silk industry of their own were unsuccessful. (source wiki)

title: What is the history behind silk production? #shorts
description:
Silk production has a rich history that dates back thousands of years. The ancient Chinese were the first to discover the process of silk production, and for many centuries they closely guarded the secrets of how to manufacture this luxurious fabric. Silk became highly sought after throughout the world, with merchants travelling along the famous Silk Road trade route connecting China with Europe and the Middle East. title: How Did Ancient Texts Describe The Process Of Silk Production? - Archaeology Quest
description:
How Did Ancient Texts Describe The Process Of Silk Production? In this informative video, we will take a closer look at the fascinating history of silk production in ancient China. Through the lens of ancient Chinese texts, we will uncover the intricate practices involved in sericulture, the cultivation of silkworms, and the artistry behind silk weaving. We will discuss the lifecycle of the Bombyx mori, the domestic silkworm, and how these creatures were carefully nurtured on mulberry leaves.

As we explore the process of silk production, we will highlight the methods used to harvest cocoons and the subsequent steps taken to extract silk threads. The video will also showcase the significance of silk in ancient Chinese culture, as illustrated by murals and tomb paintings from the Han dynasty.

Additionally, we will examine the archaeological evidence that supports these ancient practices, including silk fragments and artifacts found in Neolithic and Bronze Age sites. This evidence reveals the advanced techniques and craftsmanship that characterized early silk production.

Join us as we unravel the rich history of silk in ancient China and discover how these ancient practices have shaped the textile industry we know today. Don't forget to subscribe to our channel for more engaging discussions on archaeology and ancient cultures.

project_name_en: Sericulture and silk craftsmanship of China
title: Inside China's SECRET 5,000 Year Silk Factory The Shocking Truth About Luxury Fabric
description:
I traveled to China's "Silk Capital" expecting ancient weaving, but the factory floor revealed a shocking, intense, and primal truth about how real silk is made. Forget what you thought you knew about this luxury fabric.

Silk is more than just expensive—it's a biological marvel. I'm taking you step-by-step, from the incredible, picky silkworm (an efficiency machine with one single job!) to the powerful industrial machines that transform its nearly one kilometer long single thread into material stronger than steel by diameter.

What You'll Discover
The crucial biological blueprint of the silkworm and why a single cocoon is almost a mile of thread.

Why the reeling process involves steam and strength (it’s not delicate!).

The surprising, deep-rooted reason why China still dominates global silk production—it's much more than just "cheap labor." It’s an inherited 5,000-year ecosystem advantage.

Myth Busting: Which parts of modern silk production are still done by human hands and why machines risk destroying this perfect natural fiber.

True luxury is not just a price tag; it's the countless processes and millennia of heritage behind the product. The next time you put on silk, you'll remember its unlikely birth.

Hit the Like button if you were shocked by the strength of silk, and Subscribe for more deep-dive explorations into how the world works!

title: Silk to Steel: Secrets of Ancient Asian Innovations! | Ancient Asia
description:
"Hello, young explorers! Today, we're setting off on an extraordinary journey to the heart of ancient Asian ingenuity. Imagine a time when incredible inventions changed the way people lived. Join me as we uncover the secrets behind five remarkable technologies that shaped ancient civilizations!"



"Our first wonder is *papermaking*, an ingenious invention that emerged in ancient China around 105 AD. In the bustling workshops of ancient Chinese scholars, they discovered that by pulping plant fibers from materials like mulberry bark and hemp, they could create a smooth, writable surface. This groundbreaking technique transformed the way people documented their thoughts, making knowledge more accessible and paving the way for the books we cherish today."



"Next, let's unravel the story of *silk production*, a marvel that originated in ancient China around 2700 BC. Legend has it that an ancient Chinese empress discovered silkworms' secret: the delicate threads they produced. By carefully nurturing these silkworms, people learned to harvest their silk and weave it into luxurious fabrics. This precious material not only became a symbol of wealth and royalty but also sparked a thriving trade route known as the Silk Road, connecting East and West in ancient times."



"Now, let's journey to ancient India and China, where the art of *metallurgy* flourished around 3000 BC. Skilled blacksmiths experimented with various metals, like bronze and iron, discovering their unique properties. Through trial and error, they learned to forge these metals into tools, weapons, and intricate ornaments. This mastery revolutionized agriculture, warfare, and craftsmanship, propelling ancient civilizations into new eras of progress."



 "Our next innovation, the *iron plow*, sprouted in ancient India around 1000 BC. Imagine farmers struggling with heavy wooden plows, then suddenly discovering the strength of iron! With this sturdy tool, they could till the soil more efficiently, ensuring bountiful harvests and transforming agriculture. This advancement not only fed growing populations but also laid the foundation for sustainable farming practices."



 "Lastly, let's suspend our disbelief and marvel at *suspension bridges*, an architectural feat embraced by ancient Asians. Using sturdy materials like bamboo and iron chains, engineers designed bridges that defied gravity. These bridges not only connected distant lands but also facilitated trade, cultural exchange, and unity among  ."



"And there you have it, the incredible tale of five ancient Asian technologies that revolutionized civilizations! Through curiosity, experimentation, and ingenuity, ancient people shaped the course of history. So, young explorers, remember to embrace your curiosity and creativity. If you enjoyed this fascinating journey, don't forget to like, share, and subscribe for more exciting adventures. Until our next exploration, keep discovering the wonders of the past! See you on our next adventure, curious minds!"

title: 新编粤剧《虎符》Cantonese Opera "Tiger Tally" 2021
description:
《虎符》之《窃符救赵》是新加坡华族戏曲研究会制作由刘满钻编剧的线上观赏版粤剧。本制作集合了新加坡粤剧界资深演员出演，由刘锦煌导演，戏曲导演为欧阳炳文，卢眉桦担任艺术顾问。本制作该是新加坡粤剧演员真人表演配以影视特效的首部数码粤剧片（附中英文字幕）。此剧由龚耀祥、刘满钻和陈福鸿领衔主演，张淑莲、巢紫贞和柳德衍主演。
Tiger Tally – Saving Zhao Kingdom is a digital production by Chinese Opera Society (Singapore) for webcast. An all-Singaporean cast performed in this Cantonese Opera which employed special effects and virtual backdrops to enhance viewers’ enjoyment without compromising the traditional artistry. This opera is written by Sally Low, a Singaporean playwright, and directed by Henry Lau. Chinese opera movements are directed by Aw-yeong Peng Mun and Cultural Medallion recipient, Lou Mee Wah is the Artistic Advisor. Singaporean veteran artistes, Gary Kong, Sally Low, Philip Chan, Jeslin Cheong, Cynthia Chow and James Lau star in this opera.

故事描述战国时代秦国围困赵国都城邯郸。赵国求救于魏国而魏王则担忧触怒秦国，只让主帅驻军邺城静观其变。魏王之弟信陵君魏无忌情急之下求得魏王姬妾如姬为其窃得兵符，夺取兵权遂出兵援赵。本剧主要描述兄弟间的猜忌，君臣间的矛盾以及宫廷内的权力斗争。
Based on a historical episode in the Warring States period (475-221 B.C.) in China, the opera tells of the distrust between brothers, conflicting stances between a king and his subject, territorial encroachment, court intrigues and power struggle. Wei Wuji, Lord Xinling of Wei Kingdom, decides to save Zhao Kingdom at the plea of his sister, Lady Pingyuan of Zhao. But the King of Wei fears incurring the wrath of the powerful invaders from Qin Kingdom. Wuji needs the “tiger tally”, a military token, so as to mobilize Wei troops…

title: 帝女花之香夭 Fragrant Sacrifice 任白名曲Yam Kim Fei and Bak Suit Sin
description:
This is probably the most well-known excerpt from the most-watched Cantonese Opera in the world.  I have supplied English subtitles here, hoping that those who are not familiar with this language can enjoy it too.

I have uploaded another version before, performed by the students of these two actresses.

帝女花 is a Cantonese Opera first made into a movie in 1959, starring Opera singers, Yam Kim Fei (任剑辉) and Bak Suit Sin (白雪仙), both ladies, but Yam impersonated a man in all her movies.

The Emperor of the Ming dynasty had just approved of his future son-in-law who won the heart of his daughter with poetry. At that moment, The Ching army attacked and took over the palace. The Emperor committed suicide with his family but Princess Cheung Ping survived. To win the favour of his subjects, the new Emperor of the Ching dynasty adopted the Princess and proceeded with the grand wedding. To show their loyalty to the Ming emperor, the newly-weds take their own lives on their wedding night. The Cantonese Opera is too beautiful to be translated. Something will be "lost in translation." The Song here, 香夭 (Fragrant Sacrifice) is the grand finale of the opera, sung on the wedding night, the couple are dressed in exquisite finery. The Princess and her groom express their love for each other and their loyalty to the former dynasty, especially the late Emperor, before they take their own lives, drinking wine poisoned with Arsenic.

I have added English subtitles for my friends here on youtube. Hope you will like this as much as I do. Regards, Jeremy

title: Trump-inspired Hong Kong Cantonese opera introduces ancient art to new generation
description:
Subscribe to our YouTube channel for free here:
https://sc.mp/subscribe-youtube

Read more about this: https://sc.mp/vhc3x


A Cantonese opera inspired by US President Donald Trump is staging its third run in Hong Kong from February 1-3, 2025. The show debuted in 2019 during Trump’s first term in office. It was subsequently updated for a second run in 2021, and creator Edward Li Kui-ming has updated it again for its latest run with the introduction of new characters and a pivotal new scene to keep up with the times. Trump on Show has been a hit, with its shows sold out, including all three nights of its latest run. What’s perhaps more surprising is the number of young people who have come to watch the production. Li said his modern-day show has introduced a new generation of audiences to performance art that dates back half a century.

title: Donald Trump Has a Chinese TWIN? | Cantonese Opera Twist! | Screen
description:
In one of the most bizarre and entertaining theatrical productions out of Hong Kong, a Cantonese opera titled "Trump, The Twins President" gives former U.S. President Donald Trump a long-lost Chinese twin, throws in an alien abduction, a Zelenskyy doppelgänger, and a dance battle in the White House.

Originally debuting in 2019, the opera recently returned for a sold-out show just as Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping held a real-life phone call. With a script written by a Feng Shui master-turned-playwright, the show blends traditional Chinese opera with modern-day political satire — and somehow, it works.

We break down the insane plot, the creative vision, and how a centuries-old art form suddenly found its Gen Z moment. From wigs to war to Martian invasions, this might just be the most unhinged yet oddly wholesome crossover of East-meets-West we’ve seen on stage.

title: How Cantonese Opera Bridges Culture, Identity, and the Future | Mitche Choi | TEDxTinHau Women
description:
Before the age of 12, Mitche Choi had no idea what Cantonese Opera was. Her first class? Not her idea. But then years later, something clicked. Cantonese Opera wasn’t just an art form—it became her way of connecting with home, identity, and tradition.

In this talk, she hopes to show how this centuries-old art form bridges the past and future, and how cultural traditions—no matter how old or distant—can help us find ourselves. Hong Kong based Cantonese Opera performer, Mitche Choi is the founder of Shan Opera, a nonprofit which strives for Cantonese Opera to remain relevant in contemporary society by curating performances and workshops since 2011.

She is the apprentice of renowned Cantonese Opera performers MAN Chin Sui and LEUNG Siu Sum.

She has worked with global brands such as Cathay Pacific, Christie’s, and Asia Art Archive to integrate Cantonese Opera with various art and media forms.

Mitche graduated from University College London with a BA in Linguistics, and from the Hong Kong Academy for Performing Arts with a BFA in Chinese Opera. In 2020, Mitche completed her masters degree at Central Saint Martins in Arts and Cultural Enterprise. This talk was given at a TEDx event using the TED conference format but independently organized by a local community. Learn more at https://www.ted.com/tedx

title: 【粵劇花旦鄭雅琪再度驚艷登場 Cantonese opera star stuns again】
description:
在香港藝術節的粵劇節目中大放異彩，此次再度擔綱演出，挑戰以全官話演繹古老戲，展現其深厚的舞台功力。在「三年粵歷三百年 · 始」中，她將於連續三晚演出化身為截然不同的角色，包括排場戲專場《打洞結拜》中的趙京娘、《武松》中的潘金蓮，以及《斬二王》中的張氏，盡展傳統身段之美與精湛的唱唸技巧。從訪問可了解她如何演活古老排場戲，讓不同觀眾都能沉浸其中。

Following her repeated acclaimed performances at the Hong Kong Arts Festival, Cheng Nga-ki is back to take on classic roles and showcase her extraordinary artistry. In the “300 Years of Cantonese Opera: The Beginning” series, she will portray a range of contrasting characters over three evenings, namely Zhao Jingniang in the traditional Sworn Brother and Sister, Pan Jinlian in Wu Song, and Madam Zhang in Executing the Duke’s Second Brother.

title: The Flower Princess·Dying of the Fragrance-Electric Cantonese Opera   电音粤剧《帝女花·香夭》
description:
“The Flower Princess” was created in 1957 by the famous Cantonese opera writer Tang Disheng based on real historical events. It has since become one of the most popular and frequently performed Cantonese operas in the classical canon, widely sung in Cantonese-speaking regions of China and Chinese communities around the world until today. "Dying of the Fragrance"(Xiang Yao) is its best-known act.

Cantonese Opera is a traditional opera in the Cantonese-speaking regions of Guangdong and Guangxi in China and is listed in the UNESCO Representative List of Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.

  This film remixes classic melodies with electronic music. While highlighting the exquisite costumes, graceful gestures and beautiful singing voices of traditional opera, it also integrates Chinese dances, shadow puppetry, martial arts and other art forms to explore the expressive potentials of Chinese classical operas. By utilizing modern filming techniques and aesthetics, the production team hopes to bring new vitality to traditional operas and introduce this excellent art form to wider audiences.

title: Cantonese Opera Performances From Sing Fai Cantonese Opera Promotion Association
description:
Missed the fabulous Cantonese opera performances by the young talented actors of the Sing Fai Cantonese Opera Promotion Association earlier in Singapore? Tune in now as we put together excerpts of their best shots in a business dinner graced by prominent figures of Hong Kong and Singapore, two applause-winning public performances at the Kreta Ayer People’s Theatre, as well as a heartening cultural exchange session with Nam Hwa Opera. Dabbling the joy of learning various facets of Cantonese opera, the children have displayed grit in countless training sessions, and developed genuine passion for this time-honoured art form. title: Dec 7, 2025: Cantonese Opera in 19th-Century California by Nancy Rao
description:
Discover the history of Chinese opera theater and its influence on financial, political, social, and family life in California and beyond.
Chinese opera theater found brick-and-mortar homes with San Francisco theaters like the Hing Chuen Yuen and the Donn Qui Yuen. But troupes had already followed Chinese immigrants to mining and railroad towns, and across the American West. As Chinese theater became part of California and San Francisco culture, popular Chinese actors advocated for their art alongside appeals for civil rights. Rao draws on personal diaries, newspapers and artifacts to place Chinese theater within the everyday lives of San Francisco. She also examines the costumes, singing, staging, and storytelling that impacted mainstream reception and influenced how Chinese communities saw themselves.

Author Nancy Yunhwa Rao excavates and contextualizes the important history of Chinese Opera Theater, bringing to light the ways it became woven into the financial, political, social, and family life in California and beyond in her book Inside Chinese Theater.

Learn more about this history during this intimate conversation at CHSA. Program includes an intimate buffet reception with the author.

ABOUT THE AUTHOR
Nancy Yunhwa Rao is a Distinguished Professor of Music at Mason Gross School of the Arts, Rutgers University. She is a music theorist and historian specializing in the analysis of American ultra-modernist musical works, the transpacific history of American music, and contemporary composers of East Asian heritage. Previously, she taught at Oberlin College and has held visiting professorships at the Curtis Institute of Music, Shanghai Conservatory of Music, Princeton University, and Bard College. She has also regularly served as an outside reader for dissertation committees at universities including Harvard, Princeton, the University of Pennsylvania, Columbia, and the National University of Singapore.

Rao's ongoing research addresses music theory and the interdisciplinary fields of musicology, ethnomusicology, ethnic studies, and Asian studies. Most notably, the transpacific perspective presented in her work offers a new framework for understanding American music history, advancing multiple fields of study, and shedding light on the often-unheard voices of Asian American in American music history.

title: Barwo Channel: Online Class of Cantonese Opera - Episode 26  Acting: Basic Skills (2)
description:
In this episode we would talk about finger gestures (指法),
Chan Fut (塵拂) and Tai Tai (大帶).

Finger gestures is a very important element, which would present the pleasure, anger, sorrow, and joy of the character through the movement of fingers. Chan Fut is a versatile tool with both
robustness and flexibility.  Tai Tai is also known as the Ban Tai,  a common costume worn by martial actors of Duen Da (短打). When wearing Tai Tai, the performers must master the force they used to is, its range and angle in order to handle this piece of costume properly.

title: Cantonese Opera The Legend of White Snake: Love Shines at 1st Plum Blossom Prize Int’l Showcase
description:
On May 22–23, the Guangdong Cantonese Opera Theatre presented the classic Cantonese opera The Legend of the White Snake: Love in Shanghai, featured at the 1st International Showcase of Plum Blossom Prize Winning Chinese Theatrical Productions.

Led by Plum Blossom Prize winner Zeng Xiaomin, it centers on the undying love between Bai Suzhen, a white snake spirit, and Xu Xian, transcending human-demon boundaries. Blending traditional Cantonese opera singing with modern minimalist stage design, its spectacular scenes like "Flooding Jinshan Temple" showcase Eastern romance and contemporary Cantonese opera aesthetics to global audiences.

title: The many faces of an ascending first lady in Cantonese Opera
description:
"It’s a riot of purple and red blossoms." This is the first line that the main character of Du Liniang sang when she enjoyed spring scenes in the garden in Cantonese Opera “Peony Pavilion - Dreaming in the garden”. The 18-minute long singing, tragic and tactful, with ups and downs, vividly interprets Du Liniang's sadness. The actress who played Du Liniang, is Liang Xiaoying, a young Cantonese opera actress from Guangdong Cantonese Opera House.

Storm, a South African girl who has lived in Guangzhou for many years, heard Du Liniang's story at a friend's party by chance, and she has been curious about this dramatic character ever since. In the second episode of “Master”, she came to the Cantonese Opera Art Museum to have close contact with "Du Liniang".

title: 马来西亚粤剧名伶蔡艳香 Malaysian Cantonese Opera Artist - Choi Yim-Heung
description:
《五月雪》团队特别邀请到了马来西亚粤剧名伶蔡艳香，为电影中的粤剧部分进行指导。作为资深粤剧演员，她见证了马来西亚粤剧从蓬勃发展到衰落景况，对影片中的经典扮相和粤剧表演做出了重要指导。

The "Snow In Midsummer" team specially invited renowned Malaysian Cantonese opera artist Choi Yim-Heung to guide the Cantonese opera segments of the film. As a veteran Cantonese opera artist, she has witnessed the rise and decline of Cantonese opera in Malaysia and provided essential guidance on the classic costumes and performances in the film.

《五月雪》目前正在大马上映中，欢迎大家前往戏院感受影片中的粤剧风采。

"Snow In Midsummer" is currently showing in cinemas across Malaysia. We welcome everyone to experience the elegance of Cantonese opera in the film.

title: The Inspiring Journey from Bodyguard to Cantonese Opera Star
description:
The Unwavering Path to Her Own Stage
[ From Bodyguard to Theatrical Voice ]

What does it take to rewrite the script of your life—again and again? In this episode of Barry & Carol, we delve into the remarkable story of Lye Meng, a woman whose life defies convention and expectations. From her early days as a school dropout to an elite bodyguard serving one of Singapore's most prominent political families, Lye Meng has always carved her own path.

Guided not by societal norms but by her own inner compass, she took on roles that required immense strength and resilience—traits she would later channel into entirely different pursuits. After years in the security field and a stint in entrepreneurship, Lye Meng didn’t settle into quiet retirement. Instead, she rekindled a passion from her childhood: Cantonese opera.

In a bold move that many would shy away from in later life, she trained, performed, and eventually took her place on professional stages in Hong Kong, breathing life into an art form steeped in tradition. Her journey is one of continuous transformation—a tribute to self-belief, cultural legacy, and the unyielding pursuit of passion.

This is more than a career change. It’s a portrait of reinvention, showing us that no matter where you start or how far you veer off the expected path, there’s always time—and power—in finding your true voice.

Tune in to witness a life fully lived: courageous, unorthodox, and utterly inspiring.

title: Cantonese Opera  -  Princess Chang Ping  (帝女花)
description:
Cantonese Opera  - Princess Chang Ping (帝女花) Of The Ming Dynasty.
The Song here, 香夭 (Fragrant Sacrifice) is the grand finale of the opera, sung on the wedding night, the couple are dressed in exquisite finery. The Princess and her groom express their love for each other and their loyalty to the former dynasty, especially the late Emperor, before they take their own lives, drinking wine poisoned with Arsenic.
The Ming dynasty is often regarded as both a high point in Chinese civilization as well as a dynasty in which early signs of capitalism emerged.[
 During the last decades of the Ming the flow of silver into China was greatly diminished, thereby undermining state revenues and indeed the entire Ming economy. This damage to the economy was compounded by the effects on agriculture of the incipient Little Ice Age, natural calamities, crop failure, and sudden epidemics. The ensuing breakdown of authority and people's livelihoods allowed rebel leaders such as Li Zicheng to challenge Ming authority.
