title: Cantonese opera: Intangible cultural heritage (performing arts) in Hong Kong
description:
Watch this video to learn more about Cantonese opera in Hong Kong!

In this video, we will introduce the basics of Cantonese opera. Cantonese opera is not just a form of art or performance with aesthetic values, but also plays significant social and cultural roles in Hong Kong. However, there has been a decline in the popularity of Cantonese opera in recent years. We will discuss the current preservation efforts and challenges.

Video Producers: Chan Yik Cheong Ken, Cheng Jiayin, Chu Wing Yee, Lau Ting Hei Hayley, Ng Yuet Ting

Tags: #cantoneseopera #hongkong #heritage #culture #preservation

Chapters:
00:00 Opening
00:50 Introduction to Cantonese opera
01:52 Cantonese opera and Hong Kong
03:03 Preservation of Cantonese opera
05:40 Conclusion

title: 皇帝都不管我！徐小鳳說鄧麗君欺君！黎小田躲遠處！戲鳳啊！#丹道花神 #台灣 #江宜橋  #百令時空 #鄧麗君 #TeresaTeng #Shorts #fyp #song #Viral #YT
description:
❤️YT❤️歡迎各位新老友來到 【百令時空】
重大開播！丹道花神300集！一部關於靈魂、輪回與宇宙頻率的史詩級東方神話長卷，#丹道覺醒 #靈魂煉化 #星辰命局
永恆的世界华裔女神鄧麗君一生密碼檔案頻道4K修復 懷舊經典
有沒有一幅畫，曾讓你流淚？

我是江宜橋（百令 伴星牡丹翁）。在北美這片土地上，我用墨韻與時光對話。這裡不僅有牡丹的盛放，更有跨越百年的歷史回響。
一筆一花一世界，一管我心歌天下！#北美牡丹王  #東方美學
#藝術重生

#丹道花神 #台灣
#江宜橋  #百令時空 #鄧麗君 #TeresaTeng #Shorts #fyp #song #Viral #YT

 #YTShorts
#Vlog #Travel
 #change

❤️頻道核心專欄：
《麗君·千界回響》：獨家講述鄧麗君在北美的真實往事，從林肯中心到洛杉磯的足跡。鄧麗君一生的精彩瞬間！鄧麗君的傳承。
《宜橋書畫現場》：看「北美牡丹王」揮毫，在鄧麗君的旋律中找尋墨韻。
《丹道花神300集》創作室，《蒙紫情30集史詩》圖說。《百令江宜橋哲理大道》之窗。

❤️關於江宜橋：
北美牡丹書畫家江宜橋，字 百令，號-我者 ，號-伴星牡丹翁。出身書畫世家，齊白石李苦禪一脈嫡傳。作品獲聯合國、美國國會山莊收藏。現任百令國際藝術設計研究院院長。

❤️姊妹頻道：
想看更多我的生活日常、老歌分享與咖啡閒談？歡迎訂閱附頻道：【百令咖啡語茶】。

YT ❤️ Welcome, dear old and new friends, to “Bailing Spacetime.”
Has there ever been a painting that moved you to tears?

I am Yiqiao Jiang (courtesy name: Bailing, also known as Companion-Star Peony Elder).
On this land of North America, I converse with time through the rhythm of ink.
Here, you will find not only the full bloom of peonies, but also echoes of history spanning a hundred years.
One brush, one flower, one universe — with this brush, my heart sings to the world.

❤️ Core Channel Series:
   •   “Teresa Teng · Echoes Across a Thousand Realms”
Exclusive storytelling of Teresa Teng’s true experiences in North America — from Lincoln Center to Los Angeles.
Capturing the most brilliant moments of her life and her enduring legacy.
   •   “Yiqiao Live Calligraphy & Painting”
Watch the “Peony King of North America” in action, finding the spirit of ink within the melodies of Teresa Teng.
   •   “Dan Dao Flower Deity · 300 Episodes” Studio
“Meng Zi Qing · 30-Episode Epic” illustrated series.
A window into “The Philosophical Great Way of Yiqiao Jiang.”

❤️ About Yiqiao Jiang:

Yiqiao Jiang, a North American peony painter and calligrapher,
courtesy name Bailing, art names The Self and Companion-Star Peony Elder.
Born into a family of calligraphy and painting, he is a direct inheritor of the lineage of Qi Baishi and Li Kuchan.
His works have been collected by the United Nations and the United States Capitol.
He currently serves as the President of the Bailing International Art & Design Institute.

❤️ Sister Channel:

Want to see more of my daily life, classic songs, and coffee conversations?
You’re warmly invited to subscribe to my companion channel:
“Bailing Coffee & Tea.”

title: Cantonese Opera Encounter in StudioKT
description:
What an incredible evening at last week's Cantonese Opera Encounter! We were privileged to dive into the soul of Cantonese opera with renowned experts - Professor Martin Lau, Dr Kelly Chan, Ms Christie To and Ms Harriet Szeto - who shared their wisdom and mesmerized us with live demonstrations of this UNESCO-recognized art form.

Joined by visionaries from music, art, illustration, and fashion, we sparked a dynamic co-creation session that buzzed with fresh ideas. From blending traditional melodies with modern beats to reimagining opera aesthetics in contemporary design, the possibilities for future collaborations are endless.

Huge thanks to everyone who brought their passion and creativity to this unforgettable night! We’re beyond excited about the innovative projects and partnerships ahead. Let’s keep the momentum going—share your thoughts to explore how we can continue to Catalyse Hong Kong’s Creative Economy together!

title: Vancouver Cantonese Opera The Masterminds Behind The Awakening 20241207 at Chinese Canadian Museum.
description:
OBJECTIVES OF THE AWAKENING

In 2025, The Awakening continues its artistic development, with its premiere set for October 2026.
The Awakening is a fusion opera that:
 Celebrates Chinatown’s Cultural Legacy – Showcasing the historic Sing Kew Theatre as a vibrant artistic and social hub.
Tells the Stories of Chinese Canadian Resilience – Highlighting their fight for citizenship rights and military service during WWII.
Revitalizes Chinatown’s Heritage – Blending Cantonese opera, Western opera, jazz, hip-hop, and contemporary theatre to bring history to life.
Engages the Community – Hosting interactive workshops to involve the public in the creative process.
Bridges Generations – Connecting elders and youth through bilingual storytelling.
Educates on Chinese Canadian History – Raising awareness about the Chinese Exclusion Act (1923-1947) and its impact.
Creates an Innovative Theatrical Experience – A visually and musically immersive production that reimagines Chinatown’s past.
Join us on this powerful journey of history, resilience, and culture!

#TheAwakening #Chinatown #FusionOpera

title: TBA 2024 Performance - Scent of Thunderbolts: A Cantonese Opera Experience
description:
This performance allowed attendees to experience the vivid history and enduring contemporary presence of Cantonese Opera.  Attendees witnessed a brief demo of a performance by Starlight students, showcasing the traditions and versatility of Cantonese opera as a mode of storytelling and an active art form practiced in Chinese communities across Canada.

This performance took place in the exhibition space for TBA 2024 artist, Karen Tam.

This performance was accompanied by a workshop that was led by Alice Chan, Director of Starlight Chinese Performing Arts Centre. With accompanying Starlight students, Alice lead participants through a brief introduction to Cantonese opera, followed by a series of movement exercises using theatre props and costumes from Starlight’s extensive and culturally rich collections.

Videography by Miles Rufields.

title: Devotion can defeat any difficulties—an inspiring story of a Cantonese opera actress
description:
In Cantonese opera, where male actors usually get more opportunities, female actors face more difficulties and challenges, and sometimes have to sacrifice their precious time, money and health, if they want to achieve success in this career.

However, with great passion and devotion on Cantonese opera, Wang Kit Ching, a young opera actress, never flinches back; instead, she turns those disadvantages into impetuses to improve and fulfill herself, not only in her career, but also in her life.

Location: Ko Shan Theatre, Kowloon, Hong Kong
Date: 5 Nov. & 16 Nov. 2016
Camerawomen: Shen Yiqian, Deng Yang & Ni Bingyi
Camera: Pannosonic GH4
Edited by : Shen Yiqian

title: Reviving Cantonese Opera's heyday
description:
Recently, CJMY had an exclusive interview with Brendda Ooi an active amateur Cantonese Opera singer and actor with Perminat Opera Dan Muzikal Cina TOTALMAS Kuala Lumpur. A mother of five children, she took it up as a hobby to fill up her time in preparation for her retirement. Though a busy businesswoman, she has a passion for the arts and even drawn her husband into it.

The Cantonese opera form was imported from the northern part of China and slowly migrated to the southern province of Guangdong in late 13th century, during the late Southern Song Dynasty. In the 12th century, there was a theatrical form called "Southern style" or the Nanxi (Southern drama), which was performed in public theaters of Hangzhou, then capital of the Southern Song Dynasty. Cantonese Opera was first performed during the reign of the Ming Dynasty Jiajing Emperor (1521-1567).It was used solely for the entertainment of the Emperor then.

title: Vancouver Cantonese Opera: The Prop Master's Dream 20241102 10 min. Highlight   道具大师的尋根梦 10分钟精彩片段
description:
In November 2022, VCO produced its first experimental Cantonese fusion opera, "The Prop Master’s Dream," which premiered at the Annex Theatre, selling out two shows and attracting a mainstream audience. This fusion opera involved musical composition, scriptwriting, community consultation, and project planning, bringing together diverse performers and musicians to share the life story of Wah-Kwan Gwan, a legendary prop master in Vancouver’s Chinatown. The opera explores themes of displacement, resilience, and intercultural memories, featuring a bilingual Chinese-English production with a score that weaves together mid-century Cantonese opera tunes, soundscapes of transpacific steamship lines, and diverse blues, folk, and vaudeville melodies. In launching “The Prop Master’s Dream,” our goal is to infuse Cantonese Opera with contemporary and experimental approaches to musical composition, stage performance, and storytelling. By collaborating with diverse artists from other genres, we aim to tap into new avenues of creative expression and audience engagement. This production engages with the diasporic history of Cantonese Opera and its relationship with First Nations on unceded Coast Salish territories, sparking dialogue on Chinese-Indigenous relations, truth and reconciliation, and the healing of past injustices. We believe addressing these issues through opera can create a safe, inclusive space for reflection on intercultural histories in British Columbia. This bilingual production has the potential to engage diverse audiences and foster conversations on race, gender, identity, and politics. To ensure cultural sensitivity and inclusivity, we work closely with experienced elders and advisors from concept to implementation.

在 2022 年 11 月，VCO 製作了首部實驗性的粵劇融合歌劇《道具大师的尋根梦》，於 Annex 劇院首演，兩場演出皆座無虛席，並成功吸引主流觀眾。這部融合歌劇涵蓋音樂創作、劇本撰寫、社區諮詢及項目規劃，匯聚多元的表演者與音樂家，共同呈現溫哥華唐人街傳奇道具師關華坤的一生故事。歌劇探討流離失所、堅韌不拔與跨文化記憶等主題，並以中英文雙語演出，音樂編排融合了二十世紀中期的粵劇曲調、橫跨太平洋的輪船聲景，以及多樣的藍調、民謠與滑稽歌舞旋律。

在推出《道具大师的尋根梦》時，我們的目標是將粵劇注入當代與實驗性的音樂創作、舞台表演及敘事手法。透過與不同藝術領域的多元藝術家合作，我們希望開拓新的創意表達與觀眾互動途徑。本製作同時回應粵劇的離散歷史，以及其與未割讓的 Coast Salish 領土上原住民族的關係，激發關於中華—原住民族關係、真相與和解，以及過往不公的療癒之對話。我們相信，透過歌劇探討這些議題，能夠創造一個安全且包容的空間，讓人們反思卑詩省的跨文化歷史。這部雙語製作有潛力吸引多元觀眾，並促進關於種族、性別、身份與政治的交流。為確保文化敏感性與包容性，我們自概念至執行階段皆與具經驗的長者及顧問緊密合作。

title: 戲曲中心：小劇場粵劇《霸王別姬》（新編） Experimental Cantonese Opera “Farewell My Concubine” (New Adaptation) 2022
description:
小劇場戲曲節 Black Box Chinese Opera Festival 2022

四度獲獎劇目載譽重演　細膩刻劃西楚霸王的悵然回眸
Award-winning production shedding new light on Cantonese opera

📆 22-23.10. 2022 🕢 晚上7:30 pm
🎟️ $280, $230 (設套票優惠 Package discounts available)
📍 西九文化區藝術公園自由空間大盒  | The Box, Freespace, Art Park, West Kowloon Cultural District

🔗 購票 Book Tickets: https://wk.org.hk/3Abyrem

小劇場粵劇《霸王別姬》（新編）為戲曲中心首部委約作品，糅合傳統與創新意念，結合傳統粵劇表演程式及當代舞台設計，細膩地刻劃項羽與虞姬之情，及訴說西楚霸王項羽面對江山美人盡失之際的悵然回眸，同時創作團隊祈望能給觀眾展現粵劇從古到今的風格。此劇曾獲邀參與亞洲多個城市之藝術節，屢獲殊榮。

所獲殊榮：
2022年北京紀念中國小劇場戲劇40周年系列活動「中國當代小劇場戲劇40年影響力榜單之40台劇目獎」
2018第五屆及2019年第六屆北京當代小劇場戲曲藝術節「優秀劇目獎」
2018年首屆中國（北京）演藝博覽會「2017年度最佳小劇場戲曲獎」

「這戲的藝術水平之高是香港少見，無論在劇情編排、動作、唱詞、走位和演員配合都相當出色。」
──香港粵劇演員文禮鳳

「可以入眼、入耳、入心、入夢的品質之作。此劇武戲文做，新意迭出。唱腔以梆黃為基礎，適當融入當代旋律和技法……頗具小劇場特色。」
──中國戲劇文學學會副會長梧桐（北京）

Blending traditional Cantonese opera techniques with contemporary stage and lighting design, this ground-breaking adaptation reimagines the last moments of a hero caught between reality and illusion.

The Xiqu Centre’s first commissioned work has received rave reviews for performances in various cities in Asia.

Awards:
“The 40 Most Influential Experimental Theatre in Contemporary China” by the 40th Anniversary of Experimental Theatre in China
“Honorary Credential Award” by Xiqu Opera Black Box Festival (Beijing) 2018, 2019
“2017 Best Experimental Xiqu” by China (Beijing) Performing Arts Expo 2018

“A level of artistry rarely seen in Hong Kong. Excellent plot, acting and singing.
── Man Lai-fung, Hong Kong Cantonese opera performer”

title: 【粵劇《畫皮》（精新版） Cantonese Opera—𝘛𝘩𝘦 𝘗𝘢𝘪𝘯𝘵𝘦𝘥 𝘚𝘬𝘪𝘯 (Refined Edition)】
description:
綺麗詭艷《聊齋》經典再現粵劇舞台
一人分飾兩角 正邪爭鋒顯功架

書生王崇文路遇小女子媚娘，憐其身世，將她帶回家中暫住，並抵不住媚娘百般誘惑，與其共度春宵。不曾想，這美艷娘子乃是畫上人皮的狐妖所扮，崇文慘被挖心。崇文之妻陳蓉為救夫君，得丹霄道人指引，前往尋求狂僧相助……

有「才女花旦」之稱的青年粵劇藝術家謝曉瑩既編且演，將《聊齋誌異》中的經典故事《畫皮》搬上粵劇舞台。演出糅合現代元素，既展示傳統美學與紮實功架，又融入現代舞及動漫風，可謂風格大膽。2020年首演後好評如潮，更位列國家藝術基金2022年度資助項目名單，乃香港首次入選的九個項目之一。此番精新版情節更加緊湊，有懸疑刺激之處，又有鬼馬諧趣之味，吸引觀眾進入妖異的《聊齋誌異》世界，看怪僧、柔弱女子、狐妖如何鬥智鬥勇，傳奇故事煥發新生。

謝曉瑩在劇中挑戰自我，一人分飾兩角——冷冽自持的陳蓉與美艷殘忍的狐妖。一人一妖風格迥異，一邊展現正統閨門女子的克制自持與孤勇執著；一邊又描摹狐妖妖異惑人之媚態，可謂極大挑戰。其餘班底亦星光熠熠，粵劇名伶梁兆明擔當文武生，加上形象正氣的阮兆輝破格飾演表面嘻哈瘋癲、內在濟世為懷的狂僧一角，定為戲迷帶來驚喜。除此之外，著名粵劇音樂家高潤鴻為此劇目創作全新曲目及音樂；國家一級演員史濟華與崑曲「武旦皇后」王芝泉之女王蕾則共同擔當導演，為觀眾帶來全新的粵劇體驗。

門票現已公開發售
節目詳情：https://visit.hkaf.org/ve50ha

A classic Chinese ghost story of romance, betrayal, sacrifice and forgiveness brought to life in a Cantonese opera production with an all-star ensemble

𝘚𝘵𝘳𝘢𝘯𝘨𝘦 𝘛𝘢𝘭𝘦𝘴 𝘧𝘳𝘰𝘮 𝘢 𝘊𝘩𝘪𝘯𝘦𝘴𝘦 𝘚𝘵𝘶𝘥𝘪𝘰 is a classic collection of ghost stories written by Pu Songling and published in the 18th century. It has enthralled readers across generations with its spellbinding supernatural plots and, of all the stories, one of the most famous is “The Painted Skin”, which has inspired scores of film and television adaptations over the years. Tse Hue-ying, a prolific Cantonese opera scriptwriter and young performer herself, is the playwright of this new Cantonese opera based on the story. In “The Painted Skin”, a fickle married man named Wang has a dalliance with a beautiful young woman who is actually a fox spirit wearing a mask made of human skin. The casual dalliance soon takes a grisly turn as Wang finds out the horrible truth. After the spirit devours the man’s heart, his faithful wife pleads with a lunatic monk for help. Wang miraculously comes back to life in the end and begs for his wife’s forgiveness.

𝘛𝘩𝘦 𝘗𝘢𝘪𝘯𝘵𝘦𝘥 𝘚𝘬𝘪𝘯, which premiered in 2020, was among the first Hong Kong projects as well as Hong Kong’s first Cantonese opera to be recognised by the China National Arts Fund in 2022. This sumptuous new Hong Kong Arts Festival production, with lavish costumes, elaborate sets and spectacular lighting design, boasts an artistic team from the crème de la crème of Chinese opera. The multi-talented Tse Hue-ying triumphantly takes on the two contrasting roles of the spirit and the wife. Ko Yun-hung, a pre-eminent Cantonese opera musician who has made tremendous contributions to the art form over the past four decades, is artistic director and himself composed music for this work. Veteran actor Yuen Siu-fai, with an illustrious career spanning more than 60 years, goes beyond his regular sheng role to play the role of the lunatic. And Shi Jihua, a National Class One Performer of Yue opera, and Wang Lei, a renowned Peking opera coach, co-direct the production. Be sure not to miss this spine-chilling feast for the eyes and ears.

title: Master | Peng Qinghua: Cantonese Opera artist strives to enliven the art
description:
May 4th marks China's Youth Day.

Traditional operas serve as a mirror of the spiritual world of Chinese people. With arduous efforts, vigorous imagination and enthusiasm for operas, young opera artists have presented a number of fabulous performances for audiences.

In addition to three major varieties named Cantonese Opera, Hakka Opera and Chaoshan Opera, Guangdong also features a lot of local operas, such as a Tea-picking Opera, Lechang Huagu Opera, Leizhou Opera and Zijin Huazhao Opera. All these different varieties work together to form the treasury of Lingnan operas.

In the second season of Master, Nanfang Plus invites several guest presenters, who are passionate about Chinese opera, to have a closer glimpse of the operatic techniques of Guangdong's opera. Under the guidance of five opera masters, they will wear the customs and makeups, and practice performing skills. What will their performances be like? Let's have a look!

Peng Qinghua is a national-level actor and Wusheng (male warrior role) of Cantonese opera, featuring acrobatic moves as his stunts. It takes a lot of efforts for Peng to become a successful martial role. In order to pull off the acting moves on the stage, Peng has been practicing countless times, which is also accompanied with numerous injuries. Peng not only excels at traditional martial arts moves, but also creates his own stunt "ben teng" (literally meaning galloping). In addition to Peng, only two other actors can pull off this set of actions for now.

Characterized by handsome appearance with endless vigor, nearly middle-aged Peng Qinghua is very strong as a martial actor.

Since serving as a Cantonese opera actor at the age of 13, Peng Qinghua has kept training for two to three hours a day, no matter what the weather is like. Having been acting as a Wusheng for 30 years, he has been accompanied by a high incidence of injury and illness. Each injury occurring is likely to stop his career as a Cantonese opera performer.

In 2015, Peng Qinghua sprained the meniscus of his knee accidentally when performing in Shanghai. In the following nearly half a year, he was completely unable to bend his left leg, and for three to four years, he was unable to squat. This sudden injury almost depressed Peng, who has always been proud of his leg movements. "For some time, I felt very regretful and worried whether I could continue my career as a Cantonese opera actor," he recalled. He once considered playing a civilized role in the operas, but he never put down his obsession on playing Wusheng.

At that time, the Cantonese opera piece entitled Wu Song's Adventure at the Lion Pavilion was about to go on tour in Hong Kong SAR. For this performance, Peng Qinghua changed all his two-leg movements into one-leg ones, trying to flip and land with a single leg. In the years after getting injured, he has performed this piece hundreds of timesand has never failed to show his acrobatic moves "ben teng". There has never been another other actor who can replace him to play Wu Song for this opera. "In the past, renowned Cantonese opera artists used to say with confidence, 'If I premiere in an opera, then no other artist can outreach me to play the role; If I play other actors' roles in any other pieces, then no one can surpass me to replay them,'" Peng said with a smile. "This principle has also become my own requirement for Cantonese Opera performances."

As a Wenwusheng (civilized martial role), Peng Qinghua not only focuses on the characters and roles of the plays, but also pays attention to the inheritance and development of Cantonese Opera. Apart from studying operatic techniques painstakingly, Peng also thinks about how to make young people familiar with and fall in love with Cantonese Opera. In 2015, Fighting for the Great Tang Empire, a new Cantonese opera adapted from a popular online game named JX Online 3, was debuted. To attract more young audiences, Peng Qinghua came to visit cartoon exhibitions to get inspiration for his creation and adaptation. In his spare time, he went to theaters to watch various dramas adapted from different works. Moreover, he also visited Seasun, the workshop who created the online game, to ask the game developers for their suggestions.

In the end, the play Fighting for the Great Tang Empire succeeded in gaining a great reputation and attracted flocks of audiences to enjoy. In its national tour, nearly 50 performances have attracted about a total of 220,000 audiences to watch the shows in theatres and the performance also gained more than 5 million fans online.

title: The Young Actor's Guide to the Values of Cantonese Opera - Vocal Training
description:
From the lens of a French director, this series of 7 video segments offers a touching glimpse into the budding craft of young masters - aged 6 to 14 - from the renowned Cha Duk Chang school who dedicate themselves to the stunning art of Cantonese Opera.

This intimate behind-the-scenes mini-documentary series explores the foundational values that shape a devoted Cantonese Opera actor:

Patience & Endurance • Team Work • Vocal Training • Acting Skills • Hand Movement • Martial Arts • Costume & Makeup

Witness their passion, their discipline, and the beautiful commitment of a new generation breathing life into a timeless cultural heritage.

title: HIghlights of "Anime in Viaggio": Cantonese opera x Italian jazz  | 粵劇 X 意大利爵士樂：《心靈之旅》精華片段
description:
"Anime in Viaggio", a groundbreaking artistic creation that beautifully blended Cantonese opera with Italian jazz, was successfully staged on 14 March at Hong Kong Jockey Club Amphitheatre, HKAPA.

The production presented the romantic journey of a Chinese traveller encountering an Italian goddess, creating a vibrant cross-cultural dialogue between Cantonese opera and Italian jazz.  Jointly presented by the School of Chinese Opera and the School of Theatre and Entertainment Arts, together with the Italian jazz ensemble Yut Quartet, the performance vividly brought this cultural fusion to life on stage.

Professor Martin Lau, Dean of the School of Chinese Opera, remarked: “This production showcased the rich interplay between Cantonese opera and Italian jazz, highlighting Hong Kong’s role as a centre for East-meets-West international cultural exchange. My heartfelt thanks to all the artists, students, graduates, faculty and audience for joining us on this remarkable intercultural journey.”

《心靈之旅》於3月14日於演藝學院 香港賽馬會演藝劇院圓滿上演，演出巧妙地融合粵劇與意大利爵士樂的藝術精粹，為觀眾呈獻一場耳目一新的跨文化藝術盛會。

故事以一位中國旅人邂逅意大利女神的浪漫旅程為主線，透過粵劇唱腔與意大利爵士樂即興對話，在舞台上細膩呈現中西文化交融的獨特魅力。是次演出由戲曲學院和舞台及製作藝術學院，聯同意大利爵士樂隊跨「粵」四重奏攜手呈獻。

戲曲學院院長劉國瑛教授表示：「這場跨文化的盛會不僅展現了粵劇與意大利爵士樂之間的豐富交織，更彰顯了香港作為中外文化藝術交流中心的重要地位。衷心感謝所有參與的藝術家、同學、老師以及觀眾，與我們一同見證這場跨文化的藝術之旅。」

支持機構: 意大利駐香港文化處
Supported by Italian Cultural Institute in Hong Kong

title: Cyrano de Bergerac A Cantonese Opera Interpretation - Interview with Dr  Law Kar Ying 访问罗家英博士
description:
How did a French classic love story come to be adapted and presented in the form of traditional Cantonese opera?

Hear from Dr Law Kar Ying as he shares his decade-long journey to bring this iconic French work to the stage in Singapore with OperaWorks - Singapore!

一部法国经典爱情故事，究竟是如何被改编并以粤剧的形式呈现？

一起听听罗家英博士分享他这几十年的心路历程，他如何与新艺剧坊携手将法国经典之作搬上新加坡舞台。

description:

Hi, This is Michael,  a private tour guide. professional interpreter/translator, sourcing agent, business assistant in Foshan city, Guangdong, China.

Cantonese Opera Cantonese opera is one of the major Chinese opera categories. It is a traditional Chinese art form that originated in southern China’s Cantonese culture, and involves music, singing, martial arts, acrobatics and acting. Today it is not only very popular in Guangdong, Guangxi, Hong Kong and Macao, but also performed in Southeast Asia, Oceania and American countries where Cantonese people live. Cantonese opera was included in the first list of 518 state-level intangible cultural heritage elements, which was announced on May 20th, 2006.

History Cantonese opera was originally called Narm hei (南戏), or Nanxi (Southern opera) in the Jiajing period of the Ming Dynasty, and was popular in Guangdong and Guangxi at that time. The first performance language was Central Plains music (中原音韵), also known as Theatre Mandarin. Later, at the end of the Qing Dynasty, Theatre Mandarin was changed to Cantonese so that it could be easy for people to understand. During the Qing Dynasty, Nanxi employed “Banghuang” as the basic tone and had also preserved tones of Kunqu opera, Ge opera and Guang opera. By absorbing the folk melodies and tunes of Guangdong, Nanxi was developed into the earliest kind of Cantonese opera.

During the Xinhai Revolution, many patriotic Cantonese performers promoted the idea of fighting with the Qing Dynasty, imperialism and feudalism through their performances in Cantonese opera. They often practiced and organized performances, and meanwhile absorbed the cream of dramas and movies. Soon Cantonese opera had been enriched by the cream of dramas and movies.

Since 1949, when the People’s Republic of China was founded, the Chinese government has placed great importance on the arts. Consequently, Cantonese opera has obtained further development and many excellent operas have been performed. In addition, many new people have been cultivated to engage in this profession. In 1958, the Guangdong Opera House was founded.

title: 達拉斯粵劇社 Dallas Cantonese Opera Society
description:
2025 12 30 zoom concer聯歡晚會
達拉斯粵劇社2025年線上聯歡晚會報導

曲韻飛揚傳嶺南雅調
花衫起舞唱海外新春

2025年12月30日達拉斯粵劇社舉辦線上跨年聯歡晚會圓滿成功。晚會節目開始，首先由社長甘秀霞致詞歡迎線上觀賞的親朋好友，並介紹當晚演出是一台匯聚多元文化、凝聚藝術熱情的高水準文藝盛會，不僅有國家一級粵劇、京劇名家的精湛演繹，還有達拉斯薈童古箏藝術學院、達拉斯楊婷工作室小天使舞蹈學校、達拉斯現代音樂學院的校長和中美藝術文化聯合會的學生們參加演出，來自社會各界的文藝愛好者用才藝與熱情點亮這個跨年之夜。當然有達拉斯粵劇社藝術顧問楊樹光導演及黃淑瓊、甘秀雁與甘秀明幾位理事的賀詞與錄像。

是晚司儀由總策劃羅莉娟、關繹軒、黃美蘭、尹瑋、張雅然和甘秀霞等共同擔任。

description:
Cantonese Opera 360° is a Youtube series which aims to give its audiences a 360° view of the various aspects of a Cantonese Opera troupe so that new and existing audiences can appreciate Cantonese Opera more after watching the series. This episode starts with a very special Nanyin introduction sung by Prof. Yuen Siu Fai ( #阮兆輝) and he will also be doing the narration for the whole series. #溫玉瑜 #蕭詠儀 will be demonstrating in this episode. Thanks to Bobo 姐 ( #鄧拱璧 ), the whole series are subtitled with English, therefore “Cantonese Opera 360° is also good for non-Chinese audiences who are interested in Cantonese Opera.
This episode will be premiered on Youtube on May 5th at 9pm

This project is supported by Hong Kong Arts Development Council “Arts Go Digital Platform Scheme” and produced by 春暉粵藝工作坊

music credits: Lau Tzu Ehru  Artist: Doug Maxwell

title: 20250830 Jing Ying Cantonese Opera Talkbacks
description:
For more information, please visit: https://www.jingyingcantoneseopera.org/
Ticketing site: https://www.tickettailor.com/events/jingyingcantoneseoperainstitute/1732315

WHAT:
Jing Ying Cantonese Opera Institute presents a rare and intimate Cantonese Opera Talkbacks Show, bringing together artists, students, and community members to learn about the cultural legacy and relevance of this timeless performing art. Audiences will experience selected performances followed by a live Q&A with the performers.
* Performances in Cantonese to ensure authentic experience with English subtitles
* English discussions to delve deeper into different aspects of this art
* Live traditional Cantonese opera music ensemble
** Special access to exhibit of Traditional Cantonese Opera clothing and accessories

WHEN:
August 30, 2025, 1:00 - 3:00 PM (12:00 PM Doors open, access to exhibit and tour)

WHERE:
Children’s Creativity Museum Theater
221 4th St.
San Francisco, CA 94103

WHO:
Featuring rising talents from Jing Ying, experienced local artists, and community cultural leaders.

WHY:
This Talkbacks Show is part of Jing Ying’s mission to make Cantonese opera accessible and relevant to diverse audiences. It offers a rare opportunity for attendees to engage directly with performers and creators in an interactive, educational setting.

title: ‘It’s a mission and responsibility’: the young Hongkongers keeping Cantonese opera alive
description:
Subscribe to our YouTube channel for free here:
https://sc.mp/subscribe-youtube

On the occasion of the 25th anniversary of Hong Kong’s return to Chinese sovereignty, a pair of young Cantonese opera performers in the city say they are determined to keep working to keep the traditional art form alive. Since the former British colony’s handover, there was a coordinated effort involving government, cultural organisations and performance sectors in Hong Kong as well as the neighbouring city of Macau and Guangdong province on the mainland. has strengthened communication and worked together to get Cantonese opera on Unesco's Representative List of Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity. Today, Cantonese opera has become an almost daily activity in Hong Kong practised by troupes of different sizes.

title: Behind the curtain of Portland's historic Cantonese opera club | Oregon Experience
description:
Formed in 1942 to raise funds for China’s defense during World War II, Portland’s Yat Sing Music Club has kept the spirit of Cantonese opera alive for more than 80 years.

Now sustained by aging members, the club works to preserve this poetic, historical art form as younger generations drift away and the future of the tradition hangs in the balance.

Founded in 1942 during World War II to raise funds for China’s defense, the Yat Sing Music Club has been a vital guardian of Cantonese opera in Portland for more than 80 years.

This traditional art form blends music, singing, martial arts and storytelling, reflecting deep cultural pride and history. The club’s aging members continue to rehearse and perform in Portland’s Chinatown, determined to preserve this poetic and historic tradition despite graying audiences and few young people joining.

As modernization and fast-paced entertainment dominate today’s world, the Yat Sing Music Club fights to keep the rich legacy of Cantonese opera alive — honoring their founders’ dedication and hoping to inspire future generations to embrace this multifaceted cultural treasure.

(Captions available in Cantonese and English.)

Find more stories like this at https://www.opb.org/show/oregonexperience/

海瑞傳之碎鑾輿 方文正撰曲 生: (士工首板)海剛峰 (工)，執掌都察院 (上)，無縱無枉 (尺)； (上唱七字清下句)淳安奉調賑災忙。官拜御史聲名廣；愛民愛國熱心腸。忍見地慘天愁累累案 (尺)；(起鑼鼓) 都是嚴嵩父子昧天良 (上)。 (詩白)地轉天旋禍國邦，社稷瘡痍有孽狼，浩然正氣師包拯，護民護國靖朝綱， (三才鑼鼓白)本官 (介) 都察院海瑞 (介) 只因嚴嵩父子，罪惡滔天，已將嚴世蕃 (介) 緝拿歸案，明日開堂審訊，定作嚴懲，剛才嚴太師闖府，搗亂公堂，已將他責打驅逐，他他他，又豈能善罷甘休，正是 (介) 此時有幸懲奸佞，他朝立志 (介) 靖綱常也 (起花上句)難停熱淚看冤書 (才)捲捲狀詞控告嚴家案； 旦: (內場白)擺駕 (上唱快中板下句)深宮接訊怒如狂。父被毆傷遭逐趕；弟郎囚禁在牢房。擺駕鑾輿衙堂往；揚威耀武救弟郎。 (一才花上句)不待通傳往衙內闖； (先鋒鈸闖入介白)唏 (才) 海瑞是你 (才) 海剛峰可是你，令哀家可怒 (才) 可怒 (才) 可，怒 生: (三才白)呔，何方婦人，擅進都堂，該當何罪？ 旦: (怒介白)我呸 (才) 你且抬頭看一看，哀家是誰 生: (口白)哦，估道是誰，原來是娘娘駕到 (起慢板下句)緩步下青階，躬身忙下拜，何事紆尊降貴，親自造訪衙堂。夜初更，韶樂奏，禁宮歌舞魚龍，你應該承恩侍奉主上； 旦: (憤介)哼 (起血淚花)怒滿懷，怒滿腔，憤慨恨如狂，枉稱忠烈，上下未分，自取滅亡， 生: (接唱)為人義凜昂，正氣昂藏，不縱不枉，心堅剛方，何事使顯貴燥擾不安，(相思鑼鼓) 旦: (接唱)莫扮傻，莫扮狂，似癲喪，你太囂張，太不當，居然毆辱丞相，將我弟拘禁監倉 (三才鑼鼓白)哼，他身犯何罪，罪犯何條，你且對哀家講來 生: (口白)娘娘，你要聽 旦: (口白)你要講 生: (口白)聽到了 旦: (口白)講上來 生: (起芙蓉腔中板)(尺) 你弟郎不羈亂國綱。身居四省都督職責廣；誰叫佢擅離汛地逸京踉。 (轉七字清)偽造戰功蒙聖上；私通倭寇亂國防。假作濟貧燒陋巷；萬民燒死梅花崗。強擄民女淫邪盪；壓奪民財昧天良。 (一才花)我身居御史靖綱常 (才)拘禁亂臣繩於案； 旦: (怒白)我呸 (三才鑼鼓白)嚴家忠義，君臣讚賞，你雌黃信口 (介) 污衊忠良呀 (起木魚)我來問你，你話世蕃汛地擅離， 生: (口白)哎呀，不好了呀 (起新腔十字清)暗心慌，已成罪證，不利弟郎。保家聲，切莫千年道行一朝喪；更莫污名張越，聲動帝王。海剛峰，佢耿直為人，心硬朗；難尋錦囊妙計挽危亡。 (一才花上句)唯有蘊面改溫容，好，我利誘權鉤謀設想；罷了海大人呀， (禿唱朦朧)從來官官相敬互來往，愚弟郎佢不肖，你從便處理無妨，寬恕他冒昧不當，未盡責崗看我面上，放弟後國學士力荐報你恩廣 (相思鑼鼓攝白)嗱，你都知道嚴家位高勢重㗎啦，哎呀又何必批其逆鱗呢，我見你一表人才，只要你順從哀家一次，釋放我弟郎，我保證你加官晉爵嘅，況且，耶，或者我地以後又可以多的來往呢 生: (接唱哭屍牌子)聽貴妃聲聲作狀，可嘆她似豺狼心悍，她要洗劣跡蓋彰，她賄俺晉官賜封太莽撞，竟向俺誘逼，半轉秋波放媚浪，用計售權與欲難如你願 (順三才口古)娘娘呀 (介) 莫不是彌天大罪，也可以用爵祿嚟轉讓啩； 旦: (口古)你寧不知嚴家勢大，順之者昌逆之者亡。 生: (口古)耶 (介) 我一向耿直無私，未屑趨炎附旺； 旦: (口古)真是天堂有路你不訪 (才) 地獄無門你趕路呀忙。 (起慢板下句)海剛峰，你個小御史，區區五品院堂。嚴世蕃，統三軍，一品大員，又豈容犯上；(腔收) (怒白)我呸 (三批) 生: (笑介)哈 (起慢板下句)五品官，承王命，有志扶邦。恨奸人，慕官利，罪惡貫盈，自當捉來歸案； 旦: (收白)哎呀，好一個鐵面無私嘅海御史 生: (口白)不敢當，有何訓諭呀當今娘娘 旦: (口白)海主事 生: (口白)嚴貴妃 旦: (口白)海剛峰 (才) 生: (口白)嚴嬌娥 旦: (重一才白)我把你個海蠻子 (起快點下句)豈容海瑞太鋒芒。 (一才花上句)海剛峰 (才)我弟在囚，你放不放； 生: (起花下句)海瑞只知有王法 (才)你莫持家勢壓忠良。嚴世蕃難饒難饒他難饒 (才)海剛峰 (重一才)不放不放俺不放； 旦: (先鋒鈸三才白)嚇？你當真不放 生: (口白)有道擅捉不能擅放 旦: (口白)如若不放，哀家要 (才) 生: (口白)你要什麼？ 旦: (口白)哀家要搜府救人呀 (先鋒鈸) 生: (鑼鼓白)呔 (介) 大膽刁娘，搗亂公堂，你且抬頭一看，這是什麼的所在？ 旦: (口白)待奴一觀 (介) 代天糾察 (才) 代天糾察 生: (笑介起花下句)御筆親題牌匾在 (才)看你還敢肆猖狂。 旦: (接唱)皇皇聖眷有特權 (才)難道你敢對哀家施刑杖啩；(三批介) 生: (口白)噫 (起救弟牌子)欲欺壓清官妄想，強把世蕃平案釋放，未因勢高張怯慌，不惜犧牲，哪肯徇私昧良(相思鑼鼓) 旦: (接轉銀台上二段)唏，怒眼望鳳目睜圓激盪，動宰相棒杖，禁囚弟郎，冒犯紅妝，搧風擯浪，你不自量，小小都察衙堂，我何在眼上，撕毀案上詞狀，難絕憤恨填胸，將案椅掃蕩 (收白)人嚟 (介) 將案椅推倒呀 (介) 生: (唱白)好呀膽 (起快點下句)皇皇國法豈尋常。 (一才花上句)叫衙差 (才)動杖刑 (才)懲戒如斯狂妄； 旦: (先鋒鈸白)呀！堂堂聖眷，你敢？ 生: (口白)噫 旦: (口白)你敢？ 生: (口白)娘娘呀，你夜闖官衙，可有君王御旨呀？ 旦: (口白)冇噃 生: (口白)可有六部文憑呢？ 旦: (口白)都冇噃 生: (口白)卻又來 (介) 一無聖旨，二無文憑，與刁娘何異，衙兒們 衙差: (應白)有 生: (口白)快快與爺動刑 旦: (口白)我呸 (介) 我係堂堂貴妃，慢說是小小衙堂，就算是金鑾大殿，哀家擺上 (介) 也做得 (介) 擺下 (介) 也做得 (介) 何懼一個小小都察院衙堂呀 (三批) 生: (鑼鼓白)有道天子犯法，與民同罪，有此衙兒們，與爺動刑(先鋒鈸，三搭箭介三次，內場唱呵介) 旦: (三搭箭笑介)哈 (介) 哈 (介) 哈 (重一才搖板)可笑你身為御史律法不彰，我身穿紫綬免刑杖，皇皇國法早已列端詳，任你費盡心思亦難在擾攘 (才) 生: (口白)哎呀 (起手托)司法未可將法枉，執法難動以刑杖，佢極乖妄豈可坐視狓猖 (鑼鼓白)俺要她來得威風，走得踉忙，衙兒們， 衙差: (應白)有 生: (鑼鼓白)手持白梃，將她鑾輿打碎呀 衙差: (齊應白)領命 (打介) 生: (三搭箭笑介白)哈 (介) 哈 (介) 哈 旦: (氣介白)唏 (起戲皇叔)皇令法，難敵抗，瞠目咋舌言忘，先引退我另作主張 (鑼鼓白)今時之辱，刻骨銘心，海剛峰 (重一才) 保佑你官位永保，一門老少，闔家平安呀，人嚟，擺駕 (下介) 生: (煞板)海剛峰 (工)，存正義 (士)，除暴安良 (上)。

title: 粵曲, Cantonese Opera, 琴桃,,李淑文, 陳偉明
description:
粵曲, Cantonese Opera, Chinese Culture

琴桃, 方淑姬, 陳偉明
陳鍚炤，西樂領導
曾湘，楊琴
琴桃 is the story of a widow who became a nun and the young man, who stirs up her heart again. On a beautiful night with the moon out and the fragrance of multitude of flowers wrapping the temple, the nun sits by the candle light playing on the Qin (琴), a Chinese string instrument. A young man passes by and overhears the nun playing. He is enthralled by her music and her beauty. He introduces himself to her. After a long conversation, he falls in love with her and tries to persuade her to rejoin the human world, instead of trying to seek heaven inside the temple.

She told him that he is too young and inexperienced to understand sadness and too young to worry about not finding someone to love. He counters that human life is fragile like the flower that eventually withers and dies. He said to her that if her heart is so still and at peace, why is it her music reveals so much of her loneness and yearning. She is quite touched by his perception and sensitivity. However tradition rules and she cannot break out of the code and run away with the young man.

Another version of this opera was originally sung by 林小群 and 呂玉郎.  陳小漢 (Chan Xiao Han) directed the rewriting and music design this opera, which is what you are listening to. It is Mainland China’s most popular Cantonese opera’s and a classic. When I first heard it, I was quite stirred by the music and the poetry found in the lyrics. In addition I wanted to see if I can sing a Mainland China opera, which is usually several keys higher and sometimes challenging for opera performers growing up listening to and singing operas from Hong Kong, which has a different musical tradition. I hope I did justice to this opera and hope to improve as I sing it more often. I was very lucky to sing it with Mr. Chan Wei Ming (陳偉明) who has a beautiful baritone voice.

Some of the musical and parts of the opera that I like are:

長二王 (Chang Yi Won):
一曲雉朝飛嘆形單隻影
似孤鴻無侶
自哀嗚琴韻淒然, 未忍聽
君子青春年少, 何愁無偶
嘆孤清


癀寒遊 (Guang Han Yau) – the music delicately accompanies the soprano and solely relies on her voice to express the meaning in the lyrics.

春風得意 (Chung Fong Duk Yi):
人似野鶴閒雲
禪房裏喜清靜
上香誦經枕畔聽鐘鼓聲清
夢到神仙樂境, 風月情
己早斷那蓬萊世間湵
紅塵俗世比併

小挑紅 直轉反綫中板:
那花落(五六工)花開
     \                  X。

By putting the accent (Ban) on “工”, the soprano then goes straight into 花開. This is a bit challenging technically, but has great musical effects if the timing is good.

 Mr. Chan Xiao Hang did an excellent job of arranging the music which has good variation in the keys from highs to low and in reverse. For a soprano, this is a dream to showcase their technical ability.

title: 粤劇日 2023 宣傳片 Cantonese Opera Day 2023 Trailer
description:
【粤劇日 2023】
今年「粤劇日」將於11月26日（星期日）下午在香港文化中心和香港太空館舉行，憑票免費入場，歡迎大家參與！多個兒童及青少年粤劇團將在文化中心音樂廳演出經典粤劇折子戲，文化中心大堂則有音樂事務處香港青年中樂團即場演奏廣東音樂、應屆香港學校音樂節粤曲比賽的優勝者獻唱得獎選段及「粤劇在香港」展覽。同日文化中心行政大樓四樓及香港太空館演講廳會舉辦多場示範和講座。

節目詳情：https://www.lcsd.gov.hk/CE/CulturalService/Programme/tc/chinese_opera/programs_1632.html

【Cantonese Opera Day 2023】
The Cantonese Opera Day will be held in the afternoon on November 26 (Sunday) at the Hong Kong Cultural Centre (HKCC) and the Hong Kong Space Museum (Space Museum) this year. "Cantonese Opera Excerpts" will be staged at the Concert Hall of HKCC, where young Cantonese opera talents will perform classic excerpts. In addition, foyer programmes include Cantonese music performed by Youth Chinese Orchestra of the Music Office, performances of the winners of this year’s Hong Kong Schools Music Festival Cantonese Opera Competition and "Cantonese Opera in Hong Kong" Exhibition. A number of demonstration sessions and talks, will also be held at Level 4 of the Administration Building, HKCC and the Lecture Hall of Space Museum.

title: 【新編粵劇《竹林愛傳奇》 Cantonese Opera—𝘓𝘰𝘷𝘦 𝘪𝘯 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘉𝘢𝘮𝘣𝘰𝘰 𝘎𝘳𝘰𝘷𝘦】
description:
金像獎最佳編劇杜國威坐鎮
大老倌與實力派金童玉女聯袂
傾力打造新編傳統大戲

香港殿堂級編劇家杜國威的作品，素以感情細膩動人見稱，曾憑《虎度門》、《南海十三郎》等電影，數奪金像獎及金馬獎最佳編劇獎與提名。今次首度為香港藝術節執筆創作粵劇，由大老倌新劍郎擔任導演，而生旦則是當今炙手可熱的年輕演員藍天佑和鄭雅琪，教人引頸翹望。杜國威為這對獨當一面而又默契十足的璧人，特地編寫多場文戲武場，盡顯其唱腔功架。

《竹林愛傳奇》乃粵劇首次取材自魏末晉初竹林七賢的事蹟，情節曲折錯綜，高潮迭起，在杜國威筆下，愛情之堅貞、友情之可貴、節義之高尚、人心之詭譎，把人性多面描寫得淋漓盡致，絲絲入扣。

門票現已公開發售
節目詳情：https://visit.hkaf.org/ozprnl

An exquisite new Cantonese opera by award-winning playwright and screenwriter Raymond To about lofty ideals and abiding love

𝘓𝘰𝘷𝘦 𝘪𝘯 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘉𝘢𝘮𝘣𝘰𝘰 𝘎𝘳𝘰𝘷𝘦 is a beautiful new venture into Cantonese opera by the esteemed local playwright and screenwriter Raymond To Kwok-wai, who has previously received a string of prestigious awards and nominations. Set in the late Wei and early Jin dynasties (circa 3rd century CE), this new full-length, two-act work is centred around the descendants of the Seven Sages, a group of literati disenchanted with palace intrigue who retreat from society in pursuit of a hedonistic life. Their stories have been passed down through generations and they remain culturally significant figures to this day.

Different forms of love are explored in this emotionally charged and action-packed production with a dazzling cast of both seasoned and emerging artists, including virtuoso Sun Kim-long, and new-generation Cantonese opera artists Lam Tin-yau and Cheng Nga-ki.

Tickets now available for purchase

Cantonese opera (Chinese: 粵劇) is one of the major categories in Chinese opera, originating in southern China's Guangdong Province. Like all versions of Chinese opera, it is a traditional Chinese art form, involving music, singing, martial arts, acrobatics, and acting.

title: 粵曲折子戲 Cultural Heritage: Hong Kong Cantonese Opera(EngSub) 紅線女 受虐毒打困柴房 可憐 悲憤 尋死 頑強 頹喪 (精彩演出)
description:
(Mobile phone watching is recommended) 手機收看比較清晰
Hung Sin Nui (25 December 1924 – 8 December 2013) (Chinese: 紅線女) is a former Chinese actress and Cantonese opera singer from Hong Kong and China. Hung Sin Nui was a national treasure level Cantonese opera master.  Hung Sin Nui began to sing Cantonese opera at the age of 12. She started from Mui Heung and her first stage name was Siu Yin Hung. She took to the stage from 1939, adopting the stage name Hung Sin Nui.   She made 105 films in her career from 1947 to 2009, but the bulk was during the late 1940s and 1950s.  In 1955, Hung gave up her career in Hong Kong and joined the Guangdong Cantonese Opera Troupe in mainland on invitation by Premier Zhou En-lai, where she performed until 1961. She also founded the Hongdou Cantonese Opera Troupe where she trained and mentored a large number of Cantonese Opera actors and actresses.  She was regarded as one of the greatest treasures of Cantonese opera and Hong Kong cinema.  She was famous for her unique sweet, crisp, smooth and coquettish "Hung tone" (紅腔) of singing which incorporated the techniques of Beijing Opera, Kunqu, and Western opera singing method. She was invited to leave a handprint at the Avenue of Stars in Hong Kong. Much of Hung Sin Nui's work and documents of her career are preserved at the Hung Sin-nui Arts Center in Guangzhou, which was opened 1998 by the Guangzhou city government to commemorate and preserve her contribution to the art of Cantonese opera.
Her son Ma Ting-sing said "mother can be described as 'never abandoning or wavering, with neither complaint nor regret' toward Cantonese Opera. Whether it was in the midst of war or when the market was light, she still insisted on performing and teaching. Even when she faced 70% empty seats she still performed at will and persisted on that passion for Cantonese Opera."
紅線女（1924年12月27日－2013年12月8日），原名鄺健廉，女，廣東開平人，生於廣州西關, 中國粵劇表演藝術家，人稱「女姐」外祖父為著名鬚生聲架南，舅父為著名武生靚少佳，舅母為著名花旦何芙莲, 表妹為前《歡樂今宵》創作人胡美屏，堂伯父為同治年间创建粤剧行会“八和会馆”的著名武生鄺新華.  15歲加入馬師曾劇團。1943年於广东肇庆演出，於中國内地首次使用“紅線女”的艺名，成为“师曾剧团”的正印花旦.  紅線女在传统旦角的基础上，融入京腔、昆腔及西洋美声技法，创造出著名的粤剧唱腔“红腔”，使粤剧旦角唱腔发展到一个崭新的阶段。

title: Cantonese Opera Foshan China. Sifu Derek Frearson
description:
Foshan, in Guangdong Province, is widely regarded as one of the birthplaces of Cantonese Opera, a colorful and expressive form of Chinese traditional theatre. Known for its rich music, elaborate costumes, martial arts movements, and emotional storytelling, Cantonese Opera has been part of southern Chinese culture for hundreds of years. Foshan played an important role in developing the famous “Red Boat” opera tradition, where travelling performers brought opera and martial arts throughout the Pearl River region.

Chinese Opera in Foshan is closely connected with local traditions such as lion dance, kung fu, and temple festivals. Performances combine singing, acting, acrobatics, and martial arts, reflecting the spirit and culture of Lingnan southern China. Today, Foshan continues to preserve this important cultural heritage through opera theatres, museums, and traditional performances.
Sifu Derek Frearson

[About the Video: You may have watched dragon boat races during the Dragon Boat Festival, but you’ve definitely never seen a competition like this! Diejiao Water Town in Foshan is hailed as "China’s Waterborne F1". Dragon boats blast off from narrow waterways, speed straight into the heart-stopping C Bend, and pull off sharp brakes, drifts and U-turns in one fluid motion. There are even spectacular boat capsizing moments that draw deafening screams from tens of thousands of spectators. Beyond the thrilling races, there are Cantonese opera performances, dragon boat feasts and the unique charm of Lingnan water towns, letting you experience the most electrifying traditional Chinese festival!]

title: The 5 Styles of Chinese Calligraphy Explained
description:
Have you ever been mesmerized by the elegant strokes of Chinese calligraphy but didn't know where to start? This video is your perfect introduction to one of the world's most ancient and beautiful art forms! You don’t need to know a single word of Chinese to appreciate the artistry, history, and emotion captured in each brushstroke.

Join us as we explore the five core styles of Chinese calligraphy, also known as “Wu Ti” (五體, wǔ tǐ). We’ll journey through their evolution, starting with the ancient, picture-like Seal Script (篆書, zhuànshū), moving to the wave-like rhythm of Clerical Script (隸書, lìshū), and then exploring the three modern siblings: the orderly Standard Script (楷書, kǎishū) you see in textbooks, the fluid Running Script (行書, xíngshū) used in daily handwriting, and the explosive, emotional Cursive Script (草書, cǎoshū). We use simple analogies, like comparing the styles to standing, walking, and running, to make them easy to understand and remember.

By the end of this video, you'll be able to distinguish between the major scripts and gain a deeper appreciation for the art you see in museums, books, and even on official seals.

00:00 - Start
00:55 - Style 1
02:18 - Style 2
04:15 - Style 3
06:08 - Style 4
07:00 - Style 5
08:39 - Ending

title: Living Heritage: Chinese calligraphy
description:
Chinese calligraphy is an artistic practice of writing Chinese characters, often with a brush and ink on "xuan" paper. The evolution of Chinese #calligraphy began alongside the earliest Chinese characters discovered to date - inscriptions on oracle bones from the Shang Dynasty (c.16th century-11th century BC) in Anyang, Henan province. Over time, calligraphy gradually took shape as a form of art rather than a mere means of record. The five major styles of script, running, cursive, official, seal and regular, were born from such calligraphy.

Calligraphy is a demanding and advanced #art. The type of brush, density of ink and texture of paper can all alter the output. From brush inclination and direction to speed of writing, every twist and turn of the wrist is also calculated. Structure of individual characters and spatial layout as a whole determine its quality. Moreover, it is said that the emotions and philosophy of the writer are directly reflected on calligraphy.

Calligraphy is refined art. "Lan Ting Xu" (The Orchid Pavilion Preface), created by Wang Xizhi during the Eastern Jin Dynasty (317-420), is one of the most celebrated masterpieces of Chinese calligraphy. Its elegance and expressive brushwork bestowed it both historical and cultural significance in Chinese literature. Calligraphy is also within reach. Like the spring festival couplets that adorn doors of everyday folks, calligraphy has always been the embodiment of aesthetic appreciation for the Chinese.

Where there is Chinese language, there is #Chinesecalligraphy, and where there is calligraphy, there is beauty. The artistry is still highly valued today, for it is more than just writing, it is a living heritage.

title: Grinding Ink and Chinese Calligraphy - ASMR Sleep Aid
description:
Relax as I grind some ink and practice some Chinese Calligraphy.
I recently acquired a stick of Chinese stick ink and a small ink stone in which to grind it. This is my first attempt using it as well as my first attempt at Chinese calligraphy.

In China, calligraphy is referred to as Shūfǎ which literally means the way, or method of writing. I certainly found the whole process of grinding the ink and then brushing it onto some paper, to be extremely relaxing. There is an order that the strokes are to made in and as a beginner, I have no idea what I'm doing. In the video, I am not actually writing, but rather drawing the symbols, attempting to make them look like the ones from a printed sheet just out of the camera's view. If I do a series of these videos perhaps I'll learn along the way and be able to memorize not only the symbols themselves, but also the order of the brush strokes used to create each. Nevertheless, I found this to be very relaxing and I hope you experience just a fraction of what I felt when you watch this video.

Congratulations! In reading the description, you found the secret bonus video!
https://youtu.be/1UV4VLdne4M

My videos are best enjoyed using headphones or earbuds.

The videos on my YouTube channel portray some of the typical day-to-day activities that I do for relaxation. This video was produced with relaxation in mind and hopefully has some ASMR qualities. Too much stress is harmful to your well-being and in today’s fast-paced world, stress management is more important than ever, and so I hope this ASMR video acts as a stress reliever for those watching and a sleep-aid for anyone having a difficult time falling asleep.

If you enjoy my videos and feel like you get something of value from them, you might consider becoming an official “patron” of my channel over at my Patreon page. title: Classical Chinese poem | Spring Dawn / Mèng Hào rán | Chinese Calligraphy
description:
Meng Haoran (Chinese: 孟浩然; Wade–Giles: Meng Hao-jan; 689/691–740) was a major Tang dynasty poet, and a somewhat older contemporary of Wang Wei, Li Bai and Du Fu. Despite his brief pursuit of an official career, Meng Haoran mainly lived in and wrote about the area in which he was born and raised, in what is now Hubei province, China. Meng Haoran was a major influence on other contemporary and subsequent poets of the High Tang era because of his focus on nature as a main topic for poetry. Meng Haoran was also prominently featured in the Qing dynasty (and subsequently frequently republished) poetry anthology Three Hundred Tang Poems, having the fifth largest number of his poems included, for a total of fifteen, exceeded only by Du Fu, Li Bai, Wang Wei, and Li Shangyin. These poems of Meng Haoran were available in the English translations by Witter Bynner and Kiang Kanghu, by 1920, with the publication of The Jade Mountain. The Three Hundred Tang Poems also has two poems by Li Bai addressed to Meng Haoran, one in his praise and one written in farewell on the occasion of their parting company. Meng Haoran was also influential to Japanese poetry.

from Wikipedia
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meng_Haoran

春曉 孟浩然
Spring Dawn / Mèng Hào rán

春眠不覺曉
The sleep of spring night is so comfortable that I do not notice that the dawns

處處聞啼鳥
When I wake up, I hear the birds singing from everywhere

夜來風雨聲
Last night I heard the sound of wind and rain

花落知多少
How many flowers have fallen? (Maybe many flowers have fallen)

title: Learn Chinese Calligraphy of Metal, Wood, Water, Fire, and Earth
description:
The article is published on the Website of Vision Times at http://www.visiontimes.com/2016/06/21/learn-chinese-calligraphy-of-metal-wood-water-fire-and-earth.html

According to ancient Chinese philosophy, the Five Elements (五行wu xing) include Metal (金 jīn), Wood (木 mù), Water (水shuǐ), Fire (火 huǒ), and Earth (土tǔ).

The Chinese believe that everything in the universe, our health included, is governed by the Five Elements.

Characteristics of the Five Elements:

Metal: Righteousness, firmness, rigidity, persistence, strength, and determination. Metal corresponds to the lungs and large intestine.

Wood: Benevolence, warmth, generosity, strength, flexibility, and boldness. Wood corresponds to the liver and to the gall bladder.

Water: Wisdom, flexibility, calmness, softness, and gentleness. Water corresponds to the kidneys and to the bladder.

Fire: Propriety, dynamism, strength, enthusiasm, creativity, and persistence. Fire corresponds to the heart and to the small intestine.

Earth: Honesty, stubbornness, rigidity, patience, thoughtfulness, and stability. Earth corresponds to the spleen and to the stomach.

All these elements interact with each other according to the principles of mutual generation and mutual inhibition. Wood generates Fire, Fire generates Earth, Earth generates Metal, Metal generates Water, and Water generates Wood; conversely, Water inhibits Fire, Fire inhibits Metal, Metal inhibits Wood, Wood inhibits Earth, and Earth inhibits Water.

The Five Elements are equally important, and should be kept in balance. If they are balanced, the environment will be stable, and people can live peacefully and have good luck.

If they are imbalanced, the environment will be unstable and unpredictable, which will in turn have a negative influence on you. Chinese astrology therefore uses the balance theory of Yin-Yang Five Elements to predict people’s fortune.

The demonstration in the video was done by Mr. Hsiao Shih-Hao, president of the Moxiang Calligraphy Association Taipei, Taiwan. (Image Courtesy of NTDTV AP)

title: Chinese Calligraphy online course: Stroke Learning (1)
description:
Professor Zhou Bin from USC-SJTU Institute of Cultural and Creative Industry is the Director of Institute of International Communication of Chinese Calligraphy Culture, Chairman of United Association of Calligraphy, and NGO Representative to the United Department of Public Association.
As a prestigious professor and artist, Professor Zhou Bin is dedicated to the international exchange of calligraphy culture and cross-cultural communication, with a mission to break down cultural and language barriers and connect people from different backgrounds around the world through calligraphy, one of the world’s long and great artistic traditions. He leads the first calligraphy class in the United Nations Headquarters and was praised by the former UN Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon as a “Master of Calligraphy” and an “Ambassador for Calligraphy”. Professor Zhou has held a series of lectures and exhibitions across the East and West, including United Nations, Columbia University, New York University, State University of New York, University of California, The New School, State University of Pennsylvania, State University of San Diego, Humboldt University in Germany, Catholic University in Peru, Asia Society, China Institute, as well as institutions in Asia, Europe, and America. His calligraphy textbook is adopted across the globe.

title: Why Chinese Calligraphy Is So Difficult?
description:
Some people spend their entire lives mastering this art… 😳
This is Chinese Calligraphy, one of the most difficult art forms in the world. ✍️
👉 It uses a large brush, where even:
A slight delay makes lines thicker
A fast movement makes them thinner
That’s why it demands extreme focus, control, and precision.
💧 During practice, many artists use water instead of ink, writing on the ground.
And here’s the deeper meaning:
No matter how carefully something is created…
it eventually disappears—just like the water that evaporates.
It’s not just art…
it’s a lesson about patience, effort, and impermanence. ✨

title: How to write the character ‘福' in Chinese calligraphy
description:
The character 'fu' in Chinese calligraphy carries profound meanings, symbolizing happiness, auspiciousness, and beauty. This character is particularly common during the Spring Festival and is often used as an element for decoration and blessings. Writing the character 'fu' in calligraphy not only requires a beautiful shape, but also conveys profound cultural connotations.
To write the character 'fu' well, the first thing to pay attention to is the smoothness and strength of the strokes. Every stroke should have its ups and downs, penetrating the back of the paper and showcasing the rhythm of calligraphy. At the same time, the structure of the characters should also be symmetrical, and the upper, lower, left, and right parts should be coordinated and consistent to avoid any imbalance in proportion. When writing, it is also possible to incorporate some personal style characteristics to make the character 'fu' more personalized.
In addition, mentality is also an important factor in writing good calligraphy. Maintaining inner peace and focus, integrating emotions into the writing, can make the word 'fu' more vibrant. Through continuous practice and reflection, we can gradually master the skills of writing the calligraphy character "fu" well, and pass on this beautiful meaning to more people.

description:
Celebrate the Year of the Horse, one of the twelve animals of the Chinese zodiac, with a calligraphy video demonstrating four script types, including seal script, clerical script, standard script, and semicursive/cursive script. title: Chinese Calligraphy: Learn The Basic Strokes of Writing #chinesecalligraphy #chinese #書法 #書道
description:
Chinese characters are composed of 8 basic strokes:
1. Dot, diǎn (點)
2. Horizontal, héng (橫)
3. Vertical, shù (豎)
4. Hook, gōu (鉤)
5. Upward/Rising, tí (提)
6. Turning, wān (彎)
7. Left-falling, piě (撇)
8. Right-falling, nà (捺)

In Chinese calligraphy, we use the character 永 (yǒng) to teach each of these strokes. 永 (forever) is a character that is composed of all these 8 strokes in the single character, which makes it a very special character.

From natural materials to vibrant red seal impressions.

This video showcases the traditional process of making Chinese Seal Ink (Seal Paste), an important part of Chinese calligraphy, painting, and seal carving culture. Skilled artisans prepare and refine the materials by hand, creating the rich red paste used for centuries on official seals, artworks, and historical documents.

A fascinating example of traditional craftsmanship, cultural heritage, and the enduring art of handmade production.

title: Li Shu 隸書 Chinese calligraphy sample lesson #1 (Basic horizontal stroke)
description:
Over 40 kinds of free Chinese calligraphy model books (original rubbings)
http://www.9610.com/qinhan/1.htm (scroll down to L, S, Y, and etc.)
Complete versions in pictures or .zip or .rar files.

"Silkworm Head & Goose Tail" refer to the start and ending of horizontal strokes typically written in Li Shu. When one has learned this, the other principles of Li Shu are relatively easy as explained in the following videos.
http://www.art-virtue.com/styles/li/index.htm

Chinese calligraphy Clerical Style "Li Shu"
蠶頭雁尾 (蚕頭雁尾) 比喻隸書的橫畫起筆和橫波收筆。長畫起筆時，回鋒隆起，形如蚕頭；橫波收筆時，頓筆斜起，形如雁尾。

title: How Chinese Characters Work
description:
Go here to learn Chinese for free: https://go.mandarinblueprint.com/yt=NryURgnLdlw

If you’re new to our channel, we are Luke Neale and Phil Crimmins, the co-founders and co-CEOs of Mandarin Blueprint. Our company is focused on one mission: To make learning Chinese simple and rewarding. We do this by creating ground-breaking courses, services, and community for Mandarin learners like you.

How we got here:

2013:
We both started learning Chinese in Chengdu, Sichuan province (China). Phil got a degree in Chinese from Sichuan University. Luke went the self-study route.
We both struggled a lot for the first year, wasting thousands of hours and thousands of dollars getting nowhere.
2014:
We both almost quit, until we discovered the magic of ‘comprehensible input’. We used proven memory techniques to achieve advanced literacy in a few months, and began immersing in Chinese TV, movies, books, comics, and podcasts.
2015:
Within a year, we went from failures, to passing the highest level Chinese exam at the time (the HSK 6), and achieved B2 spoken fluency. We did this learning part time, while it should take a FULL TIME learner at least twice as long (that works out to at least 4x faster).
We became tired of everyone we knew falling into the same traps we did, struggling needlessly. We decided to share what we knew with the local community in Chengdu.
2016-2018:
We started Mandarin Blueprint. We spent 100s of hours teaching what we knew in live and online seminars, gradually developing our own curriculum.
2019-2024: We officially launched our online video curriculum, and continually improved and perfected the content, community, and various supplementary courses.
TODAY: Our paid membership Mandarin Blueprint Pro is taking the Mandarin-learning world by storm, making Chinese simple and rewarding for thousands of learners.

To our fellow Mandarin enthusiasts! Whether you’re learning this language to...

Connect more with a friend or family member,
Improve your career prospects,
Travel the world,
Massively raise your status as a human being
OR purely for fun, the challenge, or sheer love of the process…

…you are on a highly worthwhile mission. Chinese is NOT “hard”. It is MADE hard by methods that SUCK.

Our method does NOT suck.

Try it for free right here: https://go.mandarinblueprint.com/yt=NryURgnLdlw

Reach out to our team anytime: contact@mandarinblueprint.com
(We’ll get back to you within a few hours)

保重 (take care)
Luke & Phil

title: Learn 8 Basic Strokes in 10 Seconds! Horizontal, Vertical, P strokes, Dot...! #CalligraphyPractice
description:
Are you still struggling with how to get started with calligraphy? Don't worry, today I will teach you a super simple formula to learn 8 basic strokes in 10 seconds! Horizontal like a bridge, vertical like a needle, long stroke like a curved knife, short stroke like a small slide, vertical hook with spirit, fold with strength, dot like a raindrop, lift smoothly. Learn these basic strokes and you can easily get started with calligraphy and write beautiful Chinese characters!
These 8 basic strokes are:
 Horizontal
 Vertical
 Left-falling stroke
 Right-falling stroke
 Dot
 Lift
 Fold
 Hook

Once you have mastered these basic strokes, you can start practicing writing simple Chinese characters and gradually improve your calligraphy skills.
Suitable for:
 Calligraphy beginners
 People who are interested in calligraphy
 People who want to get started with calligraphy quickly

Warm tips:
 Please choose a suitable brush and paper.
 Practice more, practice makes perfect.
 You can refer to some calligraphy textbooks or video tutorials.

Welcome to InkDifferent Studio, your gateway to the enchanting world of Oriental art! Dive into the ancient traditions of Chinese painting and calligraphy, where every brushstroke tells a story and every character sings with history. Whether you're a curious beginner or a seasoned artist, join us as we uncover the techniques, history, and cultural significance of these beautiful art forms.

In this first session we will discuss a few essential topics about Chinese calligraphy and the Thousand Character Classics, and you will learn to write the title in three scripts, namely Seal Script, Regular Script, and Running (Semi-Cursive) Script.

title: Traditional Huizhou Ink Stick Craft | Ancient Chinese Calligraphy Heritage
description:
Traditional Huizhou Ink Stick Craft | Ancient Chinese Calligraphy Heritage

Huizhou Ink (Huimo) is one of China's most treasured traditional crafts, historically valued with the saying: "One Tael of Gold, One Tael of Ink." This ancient art has been practiced for centuries and remains an important part of Chinese calligraphy, painting, and scholarly culture.

In this video, a skilled Chinese artisan follows the traditional process of creating Huizhou ink sticks, beginning with natural materials and soot collected through time-honored methods. The craft requires patience, precision, and generations of inherited knowledge to transform raw materials into elegant ink used by artists and calligraphers.

Recognized as part of China's National Intangible Cultural Heritage, Huizhou ink represents the fusion of craftsmanship, culture, and history. Every ink stick carries the legacy of ancient Chinese scholars and artisans, preserving a tradition that has shaped East Asian art for hundreds of years.

Keywords: huizhou ink, chinese ink, ink stick, traditional ink making, chinese craft, calligraphy ink, huimo, artisan craftsmanship, chinese heritage, ink stick craft, handmade ink, ancient chinese art, cultural heritage, traditional craftsmanship, luxury craft, calligraphy tools, scholar culture

title: The Most Asked-About Tool in My Studio
description:
Chinese calligraphy brushes are probably the most asked-about tools in my studio. Every time I use one in a painting video or reel, someone asks what brush it is and where they can get one.
And I understand why.
Over the years, these brushes have become some of my favorite watercolor tools. They hold a huge amount of water, create beautiful soft edges, produce expressive brushstrokes, and make blending effortless.

In this video, I'll share 5 reasons why I love using Chinese calligraphy brushes for watercolor, show you the brushes from my own collection, and demonstrate them in action while painting a landscape.

In this video:
• Why Chinese calligraphy brushes hold so much water
• How they help create soft edges and smooth blends
• Why they produce unique, expressive brushstrokes
• What makes them so versatile
• Their limitations and who they might not be suitable for
• The brushes I personally use and recommend

title: Traditional Chinese Handmade Craft | Making a Jade Pen Shaft Calligraphy Brush by Hand #handmade
description:
The calligraphy brush is an essential tool in traditional Chinese calligraphy and a beautiful craft.
The artisan first cuts and polishes jade into a cylindrical shape, then carves it into a comfortable and aesthetically pleasing pen shaft.
Next, the brush tip is made from high-quality hairs, carefully washed, selected, trimmed, and bound through multiple steps.
The brush hairs are precisely attached to the pen shaft, ensuring smooth writing with proper elasticity.
The entire process is entirely handmade, with each brush representing traditional skill, patience, and a vital part of Chinese cultural heritage.

title: Chinese Calligraphy Tutorial | Strokes: Right-falling Stroke 捺 (正捺、平捺、反捺)
description:
捺，通常和撇同时使用。
捺 (Right-falling stroke) is usually used together with 撇 (Left-falling Stroke).

捺分为三种，正捺，平捺和反捺。
It can be divided into three types: positive, flat and negative.

*笔锋：tip of the brush
*露锋：When writing, the tip of the brush is exposed outside.
*藏锋：When writing, the tip of the brush is hided in the stroke.

正捺可以藏锋，也可以露锋，使用时注意与撇的搭配或衔接
正捺 can be written either 露锋or 藏锋. Pay attention to the collocation or connection when write with right-falling strokes.

平捺通常用于走之旁，人们称它的外形为“蚕头雁/燕尾”“一波三折”
平捺 is usually used in radical 走之旁(辶). People call it "silkworm head and goose tail" and "one wave with three twists and turns".

反捺像一个大大的点，一般情况下，一个字有两个捺时，为了避免重复，会使用反捺。
反捺 is like a big dot. In general, when a character has two right-falling strokes, it will be used to avoid repetition.

更多含有笔画捺的字：
For more characters with right-falling strokes to practice：
https://drive.google.com/file/d/13fllvnxFLpT8L0eis1TXwRAK-4ni6_ev/view?usp=sharing

Hope you enjoy today's video. (๑╹ヮ╹๑)ﾉ

title: How to write the character ‘龙' in Chinese calligraphy
description:
Understanding Chinese characters, starting from oracle bone script, observing the evolution of "dragon"
Dragon is a pictographic character
The evolution of the character 'dragon' has gone through
From simplicity to complexity, from differentiation to integration, from complexity to simplicity

The character 'dragon' first appeared in oracle bone script
The character 'dragon' in oracle bone script represents the shape of a beast's head and a snake's body
Afterwards, the evolution of bronze inscriptions, seal script, and traditional dragon characters followed a clear pattern
The dragon head and dragon body have become more complex

Before the simplification of Chinese characters, the strokes of dragon characters were numerous
Still retaining some kind of pictographic meaning
After simplification, it has become one of the simplified Chinese characters in modern stroke teaching
Only five paintings

title: Chinese calligraphy lesson: the eight basic Chinese strokes （English explanation）
description:
All Chinese calligraphy and painting courses https://wuchineseart.com/

To help you better understand Chinese calligraphy and painting, I created a New Youtube channal Wu Chinese Art Workshop
https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCpJrY2Lssb1fPFB3t6AYwFA

In Chinese calligraphy, three are eight basic strokes, which most of other strokes were developed based on. And the Chinese character 永Yǒng (means forever) covers all this eight strokes, which is known as 永字八法yǒng zì bā fǎ (eight Chinese calligraphy methods included in the Chinese character Yǒng). This Chinese calligraphy tutorial will give you a detailed introduction of these eight methods. This is the time-table of this video calligraphy lesson:

0:00:08.490 -- 0:01:02.910  Four treasures of writing Chinese calligraphy
0:01:14.960 -- 0:02:09.670  Demonstration of writing Chinese character Yǒng, means forever.
0:02:25.470 -- 0:03:15.829  Details of basic calligraphy stroke Diǎn, dot
0:03:10.010 -- 0:04:30.300  Details of basic calligraphy stroke Héng, horizontal stroke
0:04:20.609-- 0:05:40.700 Details of basic calligraphy stroke Shù, vertical stroke
0:05:40.700 -- 0:06:28.870  Details of basic calligraphy stroke Gōu, hook
0:06:31.840- 0:09:35.680 Details of basic calligraphy stroke Tí, slash moving up from southwest to northeast fast, and long Piě, long slash moving down from the northeast to the southwest fast
0:09:50.760 -- 0:10:48.140  Details of basic calligraphy stroke Duǎn Piě, short slash moving down from the northeast to the southwest
0:10:56.840 -- 0:12:59.640  Details of basic calligraphy stroke Nà, slash moving from the northwest to the southeast.

By the way, there are many writing styles and calligraphy styles in Chinese calligraphy, the writing style that I showed you in this video is called kǎishū, which is considered as the regular script in Chinese writing system since 2000 years ago, and it didn’t changed a lot in the past 2000 years.

title: Fine Strokes - Chinese Calligraphy Template 「字組」筆畫模板 (不鏽鋼/黃銅)
description:
This is an interactive “Fine Strokes - Chinese Calligraphy Template” writing tool that breaks down Chinese
characters into hollowed-out strokes, allowing users to creatively assemble them like a puzzle and start a new
chapter in writing practice.
The design transforms strokes like "dot, horizontal, vertical, slant and forceful press" into precisely cut hollow tracks on a metal plate.
This design brings writing back to its purest tactile form, allowing everyone to become a craftsman of Chinese characters and discover the beauty of Chinese in a fun way.
這是一款以筆畫為核心的金屬互動習字工具。我們相信，再複雜的中文字，皆由基本筆畫所建構。設計將「點、橫、豎、撇、捺」等筆劃，化為精密切割於金屬板上的鏤空軌跡。這為中文教學與自學增加玩味，將枯燥的臨摹，轉化為充滿有趣的探索之旅。

此設計，讓書寫回歸最純粹的觸覺，讓每個人都能成為漢字的鑄造師，在樂趣中領悟中文之美。

title: O segredo dos mestres da caligrafia chinesa. 👀 #curiosidades #curioso
description: Você já imaginou treinar uma das artes mais difíceis do mundo usando apenas água? 😳 Neste vídeo, mostramos como os mestres da caligrafia chinesa passam anos aperfeiçoando cada movimento com um pincel extremamente sensível, capaz de transformar até a menor tremidinha em um erro visível. Para evitar desperdiçar tinta e papel, eles praticam escrevendo no chão com água, que desaparece em poucos minutos, permitindo repetir os traços quantas vezes forem necessárias. É uma técnica simples, mas que exige muita paciência, concentração e disciplina. ✨🖌️ Gostou dessa curiosidade? Learn Mandarin Chinese Character, Pinyin Pronunciation, Stroke Order, Meaning, and Example Sentences.
Learn Mandarin Chinese Language Basic Characters and Daily Conversations

——————————————
You may think learning Chinese is difficult since there are tens of thousands of Chinese characters, but actually only around 1,000 words are most frequently used in daily conversations.

So where to start? Well, here are the most useful Chinese characters you need to know. We show these characters with writing stroke order, pinyin, meaning and example sentences.

Please note that the pronunciations in the videos are Mandarin Chinese (using Pinyin romanization) and simplified characters are used instead of traditional.

Modern Mandarin Chinese has many homophones. The same spoken syllable may be represented by many characters, depending on meaning. A single character may also have a range of meanings, or sometimes quite distinct meanings.

Chinese is not alphabetic so the writing is not related to its phonetics. There are many styles of Chinese writing and All the Chinese characters on our site are Kaiti (the standard style) in the simplified form.

Learning to write and read in Chinese takes quite a lot of time and effort, but it’s not as hard as it might seem at first. Hopefully our videos will make the journey a bit easier. Start from the most basic and commonly used Chinese Characters above as you start your Chinese Learning journey. These Characters are very simple with only a few strokes. You’ll find them easy to remember as well, since some of the characters actually resemble their meaning.

——————————————
For more Free Chinese Leaning Courses, Please Check Our Website http://www.echineselanguage.com/. We aim to provide free materials of Basic Chinese Characters, Daily Chinese Conversations, Chinese Alphabet Pinyin, Chinese Numbers, Chinese Listening Practice, and more.

Chinese Language, also called Chinese Han, includes a variety of dialects.  Mandarin Chinese is the official language of the Mainland China and Taiwan, and one of the official languages of Singapore. Modern Standard Chinese is also one of the six official languages of the United Nations.

Many people start to realize that learning Chinese Mandarin is getting more and more popular and useful, no matter you are a school student, traveling in China, or dealing business with Chinese.

Actually, Learning Chinese Mandarin is never going to be easy, just as any of the second language. Mandarin Chinese use tones to indicate differences of meaning between words or syllables. Modern Standard Chinese has four tones and Chinese words often have only one syllable.

title: Wu Shanzhuan: A Revolution in Interpretation of Chinese Calligraphy
description:
Today I talk about Wu Shanzhuan, one of the most prominent figures of the 1980s Conceptual art movement in China and a pioneer of incorporating textual pop references into his art.

 His art is Conceptual in nature, often dealing with issues surrounding language. He is known for his experimental works with language and the use of big character posters inspired by propaganda used by the People’s Republic of China in the 60’s. In this era when Chinese conceptual art generally faced a rethink, his works attest a vibrancy of concept and the power of thoughts.

Enjoy!

———

I purchased a Chinese calligraphy ink on AlliExpress and in this video I'm testing it if they are waterproof, how they react on watercolor paper or my favorite paper, Royal Talens Art Creation sketchbook!

title: Chinese Calligraphy for Lunar New Year | Writing Spring Festival Couplets Part 2
description:
A calm and immersive calligraphy session featuring the writing of traditional Chinese Spring Festival couplets to celebrate the Lunar New Year.
Filmed in real time, focusing on brush movement, ink flow, and the rhythm of Chinese calligraphy.

his video captures a traditional Chinese calligraphy moment during the Lunar New Year, featuring the writing of Spring Festival couplets (Chunlian).

Spring Festival couplets are an important part of Chinese New Year celebrations. Written with brush and ink on red paper, they express wishes for good fortune, prosperity, and happiness in the coming year.

In this video, you will see the complete calligraphy process — from brush control and stroke order to the natural flow of ink and structure of each character. The recording focuses on the writing itself, creating a quiet and immersive atmosphere.

This video is suitable for viewers interested in:
– Chinese calligraphy
– Traditional Chinese culture
– Lunar New Year traditions
– Relaxing and meditative art videos

title: They Thought Chinese Calligraphy Was Simple.. But It Wasn't Easy!
description:
Chinese art brushes have been used for thousands of years. 🖌️✨
They are an important part of traditional Chinese art. 🇨🇳🎨
These brushes are used for painting and calligraphy. ✍️🖼️
Artists create beautiful landscapes with them. 🌄🌿
They can also paint flowers and animals. 🌸🐦
The brushes are made from soft natural hair. 🐐🪶
Different brushes create different effects. 🎭✨
Some brushes make thin lines. 📏🖌️
Others make thick and bold strokes. 💪🎨
Chinese calligraphy is considered a respected art form. 📜✨
A single brush can create many styles. 🎨🖌️
Artists spend years mastering brush techniques. ⏳👨‍🎨
The brush responds to pressure and movement. 🤲✨
Traditional ink is often used with these brushes. ⚫🖌️
Chinese brush painting focuses on simplicity. 🌿🎨
Every stroke has meaning and purpose. ✨📜
The art reflects nature and culture. 🌏🇨🇳
Brushes come in many shapes and sizes. 📏🖌️
Some are designed for detailed work. 🔍🎨
Others are perfect for large paintings. 🖼️✨
Chinese art continues to inspire artists worldwide. 🌎🎨
Many museums display famous brush paintings. 🏛️🖼️
The tradition is still alive today. ❤️🇨🇳
Learning brush painting improves creativity. 💡🎨
Chinese art brushes remain a symbol of artistic excellence. 🖌️🏆

title: This Brush Exposes Every Mistake
description:

This Brush Exposes Every Mistake 😳🖌️


At first glance, this looks like a normal brush...

But for calligraphy artists, it reveals every tiny mistake 👀

In Chinese Calligraphy, even the smallest movement of the hand can completely change the final result.

🖌️ A slight change in pressure...

🖌️ A tiny shift in angle...

🖌️ A stroke that's just a little too long...

And suddenly the character can look wrong—or even take on a different meaning.

That's why control is everything.

Before creating real artwork, many students spend months practicing with water on the ground.

💧 No ink.

💧 No paper.

Just repetition and precision.

The water quickly evaporates, allowing them to practice again and again without wasting materials.

Only after mastering brush control can they create characters that are balanced, elegant, and accurate.

✨ In calligraphy, the brush doesn't hide mistakes...

It exposes them.

※说明：
中国书法是世界唯一集合文字和艺术为一身的存在，不管篆刻，字画，还是现代AI时代，都离不开它。会中国字的人，占世界三分之一，就母语而言，中国文字的影响无疑是深远的。 汉字是世界上最完善的文字 没有之一。
《写个阿嬷的情书》中有很多写信的场景，直白粗粝的心意借由先生的手就染上了时间与文化的温柔。这一小段在电影院看到的时候不由地想起张九龄的“海上升明月，天涯共此时。情人怨遥月，竟夕起相思。”

title: The art of Chinese seal engraving
description:
UNESCO: Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity - 2009
URL: http://www.unesco.org/culture/ich/RL/00217
Description: The art of seal engraving is a cornerstone of Chinese fine arts. The seal was originally used as a signature or sign of authority, but it came to be used by all social classes and in much of Asia. The Seal Engravers Society of Xiling in Zhejiang Province, central China, which was founded a century ago, preserves the art of seal engraving along with approximately a hundred other specialized institutions. The design is first sketched on paper, and then engraved on stone, in reverse, with a knife. In addition to mastery of traditional calligraphy, the art of engraving requires a high degree of virtuosity, since the artist works on a tiny surface area where every curve, every thickness of line counts. The very diverse motifs are the fruit of the artists imagination and culture. As an instrument of calligraphy and painting, the seal is a work of art in itself. It expresses an entire cultures ideas about humankind and nature. Today, seals continue to be used in official documents and private correspondence. Even though those understanding the complex characters are ever fewer, the art of seal engraving is still practised by both professionals and amateurs.
Country(ies): China

title: Chinese Seal Engraving
description:
As a cornerstone of Chinese fine arts, Chinese seal engraving manifests the beauty of Chinese characters to the utmost.
        From the ancient imperial seals made of jade, to the more common seals used by all, history unfolds itself through these intricate curves and lines.
        The art of engraving requires mastery of calligraphy and a high degree of virtuosity.
        The imperial jade seals were used by the Chinese emperors as a sign of power and authority.
        Over 2000 years ago, China’s first imperial seal was created for China’s first emperor Qin Shi Huang. This Heirloom Seal of the Realm was carved out of Heshibi, a sacred piece of jade.
        Later, the seals become more widely used in lieu of signatures and as artistic collections.
        Today, Chinese seal engraving still enjoys great popularity.
        It helps improve one’s concentration and observation skills, also enabling the development of aesthetic taste.
        Consisting of the Chinese character “Jing” which also portrays a human figure running forward and embracing triumph, the emblem of the 2008 Beijing Olympic Games was also inspired by this art.
        Chinese seal engraving is a combination of craftsman spirit and classical Chinese aesthetics, endowed with the wisdom of ancient Chinese people and the value of Chinese culture.
        Producer  Yu Lan
        Executive Producer  Wu Qingcai  Qi Bin
        Coordinator  Wang Kai  Zeng Nai

title: The Art of Chinese Seal Engraving (1/2) making of an Artist Penname Seal for Lina in Russia
description:
And if you want to order your personalized Chinese name seal on soapstone please visit https://www.blueheronarts.com/custom-design-carving-name-seal-mood-image-seals-p-12555.html

The Part 1of this video series shows some useful websites and the computer software in the process of creating an artist penname seal for Galina in Moscow.
1. Get a Chinese name software at
http://www.mandarintools.com/
2. Online Chinese Etymology online dictionary at
http://www.chineseetymology.org/
3. Chinese Name Seal Generator:
http://www.makepic.com/print.php
4. The largest Chinese Seal Database
http://www.sealbank.net
5.The Yale website where you can download the fonts: http://www.yale.edu/chinesemac/pages/fonts.html



Thanks!

Henry
